result.chapter_summary: Chapter 4 argues that American education is controlled by an insulated 'Thoughtworld'—a progressive ideological consensus that makes traditional pedagogical ideas literally unthinkable. Hirsch asserts that this thoughtworld protects itself from failure by blaming external social factors or improper implementation rather than its own foundational doctrines, which are rooted more in romantic cultural attitudes than in mainstream science.

claims.text: Educators persist in advocating anti-fact, anti-rote-learning, and anti-verbal practices despite these practices having put American education at risk.
claims.source_passage: Why do educators persist in advocating the very antifact, anti-rote-learning, antiverbal practices that have led to poor results—persist in urging them, indeed, even more intensively than before?

claims.text: Within the current American educational community, there is no 'thinkable' alternative to progressive doctrine.
claims.source_passage: The basic answer is this: Within the educational community, there is currently no thinkable alternative.

claims.text: The long dominance of anti-traditional rhetoric in teacher-training institutions has ensured that competing, nonprogressive principles are not readily available.
claims.source_passage: The long dominance of antitraditional rhetoric in our teacher-training institutions has ensured that competing, nonprogressive principles are not readily available within their walls.

claims.text: No professor at an American education school will advocate for pedagogy that is pro-rote-learning, pro-fact, or pro-verbal.
claims.source_passage: No professor at an American education school is going to advocate pro-rote-learning, pro-fact, or pro-verbal pedagogy.

claims.text: Questioning progressive doctrine is perceived as putting the identity of the education profession itself in doubt.
claims.source_passage: To question progressive doctrine would be to put in doubt the identity of the education profession itself.

claims.text: The foundational premise of the education profession is that progressive principles are inherently right and therefore cannot cause educational ineffectiveness.
claims.source_passage: Its foundational premise is that progressive principles are right. Being right, they cannot possibly be the cause of educational ineffectiveness.

claims.text: Standardized tests are often disparaged by educators because the results inhibit the promotion of progressive educational doctrine.
claims.source_passage: A subcategory of this position is the denial of test results: the tests are not assessing true outcomes in the schools and are, in fact, inhibiting true education.

claims.text: When schools fail, the educational community often blames external social problems rather than faulty doctrine.
claims.source_passage: Shortcomings are to be attributed not to faulty doctrine but to overwhelming social problems, like broken families, the culture of violence, a multiethnic student population, and the demand for schools to do too many different things.

claims.text: Current educational reform often consists of 'homeopathic' doses of the same progressive principles that caused the problems in the first place.
claims.source_passage: In sum, since progressive doctrine cannot be at fault, the only proper cure for our ailing schools is homeopathic “reform,” that is, even stronger doses of progressive principles administered even more intensively.

claims.text: Historical description is the best way to initiate 'break-the-mold' thinking among education professors.
claims.source_passage: Historical description is probably the best way to initiate such “break-the-mold” thinking. Perhaps an understanding of the historical origins of progressive ideas might begin to help overthrow their feckless tyranny.

claims.text: Mainstream science is more likely to be correct than the specific science cited by educational experts because it is more diversified and disinterested.
claims.source_passage: Yet when there is a clear conflict between widely accepted mainstream science and the science cited by educational experts, it seems reasonable to conclude that mainstream science, being more diversified and disinterested, is more likely to be correct.

claims.text: American educational doctrines are rooted in deep-seated national cultural attitudes rather than just professional self-interest.
claims.source_passage: Much more than institutional ideology has been at work in forming dominant American educational ideas, many of which belong to our culture generally. Views about early childhood, book learning, creativity, independent-mindedness, and school control belong both to the educational community and to received American culture.

claims.text: American educational ideas are influenced by self-interested ideology, particularly in their emphasis on process over content.
claims.source_passage: It is true that American educational ideas are not completely free from self-interested ideology, particularly in their self-aggrandizing stress on process.

claims.text: Dominant American educational ideas are deeply rooted in general American culture, not just institutional ideology.
claims.source_passage: Much more than institutional ideology has been at work in forming dominant American educational ideas, many of which belong to our culture generally.

claims.text: Deep-rooted national attitudes have caused certain educational doctrines to persist in the United States longer than in other nations.
claims.source_passage: Deep-rooted national attitudes have caused certain educational doctrines to take hold in the United States much more persistently than elsewhere.

claims.text: Johann Herbart influenced the direction of modern American education more significantly than Thomas Jefferson did.
claims.source_passage: On the other hand, Herbart was not a schoolmaster either, yet more than Jefferson, alas, he did greatly influence the direction taken by modern American education.

claims.text: European Romanticism created the conception of the child that dominates modern American educational theory.
claims.source_passage: It was European Romanticism that created the new conception of the child which came to dominate American educational theory.

claims.text: Before Romanticism, education was based on the assumption that a child's instinctual impulses must be molded to the ways of society.
claims.source_passage: Before the Romantic Movement, the underlying conception of education... assumed that a child is a still-to-be-formed creature whose instinctual impulses need less to be encouraged than to be molded to the ways of the society in which it is growing up.

claims.text: Plato believed a just society requires the rational parts of human nature to control instinctive and emotional aspects.
claims.source_passage: Plato, for instance, attached high importance to education because he was convinced that a good life and a just society required the special training and encouragement of the rational parts of human nature so they would dominate and control its instinctive, emotional aspects.

claims.text: From a Platonic perspective, education that allows instinct to dominate reason is the root of evil.
claims.source_passage: For Plato, the root of evil would be an education that allowed instinct and emotion to dominate over reason.

claims.text: Judeo-Christian educational theory is characterized by a distrust of human nature and instinctive emotions.
claims.source_passage: Likewise, the Judeo-Christian theory of education, while stressing rationality less than Plato did, discloses a similar distrust of human nature and of its instinctive emotions.

claims.text: The doctrine of original sin implies that human nature is corrupt from birth and must be corrected rather than followed.
claims.source_passage: The pear tree episode said to Augustine that human nature is corrupt from birth. The aim of education is not to follow human nature but to correct it, to set it on a path of virtue that is often contrary to its natural development.

claims.text: Allowing fallen natural instincts to develop freely leads to greed, selfishness, and crime.
claims.source_passage: To give one’s fallen natural instincts free rein would beget a life of greed, selfishness, and crime.

claims.text: The thinkers of the Enlightenment generally maintained the molding and civilizing principles of education despite breaking with sectarian religion.
claims.source_passage: Although the thinkers of the Enlightenment broke with sectarian religion and the idea of original sin, most of them did not break with the molding and civilizing principles of education that had animated societies through history.

claims.text: The American founders, including Madison, Jefferson, and Hamilton, held a skeptical and suspicious view of human nature.
claims.source_passage: The founders of the United States—Enlightenment figures like Madison and Jefferson (and emphatically their colleague Hamilton, memorialized on the Teachers College frieze)—took a skeptical and suspicious view of human nature.

claims.text: The US Constitution was designed to protect against the innate flaws of human nature rather than trust in innate goodness.
claims.source_passage: The Constitution they framed does not imply trust in the innate goodness of human nature when allowed to follow its bliss.

claims.text: Child-centered education is a direct legacy of the 'Herbart Society' and the Romantic movement.
claims.source_passage: Johann Herbart... eponym of the Herbart Society, which fostered “child-centered education”... flourished in Switzerland and Germany during the height of the Romantic Movement.

claims.text: Thomas Jefferson's educational philosophy focused on cultivating talent and virtue through discipline rather than natural development.
claims.source_passage: In Jefferson’s writings on public education, which are the fullest we have from the Founding Fathers, the stress on cultivating an aristocracy of talent and virtue, as well as the stringent rules for moral education, does not disclose a confidence that human nature should be encouraged to follow its natural development.

claims.text: Jefferson believed the study of history was the primary subject for preparing citizens to judge and defeat political ambition.
claims.source_passage: The study of history (not nature) was to be the main subject of education for the people: [It] will qualify them as judges of the actions and designs of men; it will enable them to know ambition under every disguise it may assume; and knowing it to defeat its views.

claims.text: European Romanticism introduced the idea that human nature is innately good and should follow its natural course without social imposition.
claims.source_passage: Romanticism believed that human nature is innately good, and should therefore be encouraged to take its natural course, unspoiled by the artificial impositions of social prejudice and convention.

claims.text: Romanticism redefined the child as a unique, holy being with trustworthy impulses rather than an ignorant adult or formless clay.
claims.source_passage: Second, Romanticism concluded that the child is neither a scaled-down, ignorant version of the adult nor a formless piece of clay in need of molding, rather, the child is a special being in its own right with unique, trustworthy—indeed holy—impulses that should be allowed to develop and run their course.

claims.text: The Romantic conception of the child is rooted in a quasi-religious faith in everything 'natural' as a divine emanation.
claims.source_passage: Behind this momentous shift in attitude toward the trustworthiness of human nature lay the quasi-religious faith that Romantic thinkers placed in everything “natural.”

claims.text: Artificial culture and society are viewed by Romantics as the primary causes of a child's moral straying.
claims.source_passage: It is only the separation from that source through the artificial impositions of human culture and society which can lead the child astray.

claims.text: Friedrich Froebel, the father of American early education, argued that instruction should be passive and observant rather than prescriptive.
claims.source_passage: Education and instruction should from the very first be passive, observant, protective, rather than prescribing, determining, interfering.

claims.text: The sentiment that social custom and fashion corrupt leads directly to the idea that pressing book learning upon young children prematurely is unhealthy and harmful.
claims.source_passage: From the sentiment that social “custom” and “fashion” (die Mode) deaden and corrupt, it is not a far step to the idea that it is unhealthy, unnatural, and harmful to press book learning upon young children prematurely.

claims.text: European Romanticism contributed the specific idea to American thought that civilization has a corrupting rather than an uplifting effect on the young child.
claims.source_passage: The idea that civilization has a corrupting rather than a benign, uplifting, virtue-enhancing effect on the young child is a distinct contribution of European Romanticism to American thought.

claims.text: The Romantic view of the instinctive holiness of the child naturally results in the disparagement of intellectual content.
claims.source_passage: It is an educational view that can glide easily into disparagement of book learning and into anti-intellectualism.

claims.text: The Romantic contrast between the instinctive holiness of the child and the corruption of custom easily facilitates anti-intellectualism and the disparagement of book learning.
claims.source_passage: The contrast between the instinctive holiness of the child and the corrupting principle of custom and civilization is a conception for which Romanticism deserves full credit. It is an educational view that can glide easily into disparagement of book learning and into anti-intellectualism.

claims.text: American intellectual traditions are divided between an Enlightenment-based suspicion of human nature and a Romantic trust in natural affection.
claims.source_passage: In the United States, our intellectual traditions include on the one side Cotton Mather, Jefferson, Madison, and Hamilton, with their suspicions of instinctive human nature, and on the other, Emerson, Thoreau, and Whitman, with their trust in the “holiness of the heart’s affections.”

claims.text: There is a deeply rooted sentiment in American education that whatever occurs naturally is automatically right and possesses an inner necessity.
claims.source_passage: It is by now a very deeply rooted sentiment in American education to think that what is natural works automatically for the good. Everything done naturally has an inner necessity and rightness.

claims.text: The Romantic optimism of American culture induces a trust in natural development and a corresponding suspicion of discipline and bookish hard work.
claims.source_passage: In education, this optimistic cast of mind induces trust in the child’s natural development, and suspicion of harsh discipline, bookish hard work, and other forms of artificial stimulation and constraint.

claims.text: William Heard Kilpatrick did not move beyond Romanticism but merely translated Romantic faith into secular, scientific-sounding language.
claims.source_passage: But in fact Kilpatrick simply translated Romantic optimism about human nature and natural development into secular, scientific language. The technical translation was verbal; behind it lay the Romantic faith that things will work out better in education if the child’s development is allowed to run its untrammeled course in a naturalistic setting.

claims.text: In American educational culture, the arrangement of classroom desks serves as a defining symbol of the conflict between free modern education and constraining traditional education.
claims.source_passage: As a result of this Romantic cast of mind, the placement of school desks in the classroom became a defining issue in American educational culture. Placing the desks in semicircles or helter-skelter symbolized the triumph of good, free, modern education over inhumane, artificial, constraining traditional education.

claims.text: The primary aim of education and civilization is to guide and shape human nature toward humane ends, rather than simply following nature's course.
claims.source_passage: The aim of civilization, and by consequence of education, is less to follow nature than to guide it toward humane and worthy ends.

claims.text: European Romanticism's influence on American culture and education is an Enlightenment-era mistake that needs to be corrected.
claims.source_passage: In the annals of recorded thought, European Romanticism, with its (alas) powerful influence on American culture and education, has been a post-Enlightenment aberration, a mistake we need to correct.

claims.text: Historical evidence does not justify the Romantic belief that human affairs are brought right by letting them take their natural course.
claims.source_passage: History, including the recent history of American education, shows that human affairs are rarely brought right by letting them take their natural course. Human nature, to the extent that it can be known, should not be left to its own devices.

claims.text: The conflict between Enlightenment discipline and Romantic naturalism is a fundamental, recurring tension in American culture.
claims.source_passage: Although the United States was born in the Enlightenment, it was bred in Romanticism. The conflict in American culture between the two traditions is wonderfully memorialized in Huckleberry Finn in the standoff between Aunt Sally... and Huck Finn.

claims.text: The United States was born in the Enlightenment but bred in Romanticism.
claims.source_passage: Although the United States was born in the Enlightenment, it was bred in Romanticism.

claims.text: Human history contains little evidence to justify the Romantic faith in natural development over civilization.
claims.source_passage: But there is little in human history to justify his Romantic faith.

claims.text: Tragic historical events like the Vietnam War and genocidal conflicts have qualified the Romantic faith in the inherent goodness of the child.
claims.source_passage: this drumbeat of tragic experience has tended to qualify our antitragic, Romantic faith in the inherent goodness and dependability of the human child when allowed to follow its own development.

claims.text: Almost all dominant American ideas and phrases regarding early education are traceable to the Romantic Movement.
claims.source_passage: The underlying tenets of the Romantic Movement have proved highly durable in the United States; almost all our dominant ideas and phrases pertaining to early education are traceable to it.

claims.text: The concepts of developing a child’s creativity and imagination originated specifically with Romanticism.
claims.source_passage: The idea of developing a child’s creativity and imagination originated with Romanticism.

claims.text: The ideal of educating the 'whole child' derives from Romantic pantheism rather than scientific psychology.
claims.source_passage: The ideal of educating not just the intellect but the “whole child,” including the imagination, derives initially from Romantic pantheistic faith in the unerring goodness of whatever comes forth from the child without artificial hindrance.

claims.text: The shift from McGuffey Readers to modern stories in early childhood curriculum represents a move away from moral training toward a focus on imagination.
claims.source_passage: The change in the early childhood curriculum from the moral tales of the McGuffey Readers to the myths, fairy tales, and stories of the present day has marked a shift in emphasis from molding the child’s moral sense to bringing out the child’s creativity and imagination.

claims.text: Progressivism achieved complete dominance in public schools in the 1950s following the retirement of traditional educators.
claims.source_passage: the doctrine emerged victorious in the public schools in the 1950s, when growing numbers of traditional educators retired and progressives moved in as principals and superintendents.

claims.text: The dramatic decline in SAT scores in the 1960s was the manifestation of a full generation of students educated under Romantic progressivism.
claims.source_passage: Thereafter, it took a full generation of progressive students... before the full effects of Romantic progressivism manifested themselves in the graduating seniors of the 1960s—the time of the dramatic decline in SAT scores.

claims.text: Romantic developmental ideas are the most damaging ideas in the educational Thoughtworld regarding academic quality and equity.
claims.source_passage: But it can be argued that Romantic developmental ideas... are, of all the ideas in the Thoughtworld, the ones which have been most damaging to the quality and, above all, to the equity of our public education.

claims.text: The educational community views any challenge to Romantic progressivism as heresy to be resisted with religious fervor.
claims.source_passage: Any challenge to them is regarded as heretical and is resisted by that community with an almost religious fervor; that is not surprising, considering their quasi-religious origins.

claims.text: The educational community falsely uses the phrase 'research has shown' to justify the withholding of academic content.
claims.source_passage: According to the educational community, “research has shown” that untimely interventions and constraints are “developmentally inappropriate” and create a hothouse, forced-feeding atmosphere.

claims.text: Romantic developmentalism has encouraged an extremely negative attitude toward teaching young children letters, phonics, arithmetic, and factual information.
claims.source_passage: This Romantic point of view has encouraged an extremely negative attitude toward teaching very young children letters and phonics, arithmetic, and factual information.

claims.text: The educational community asserts that untimely interventions in early childhood are 'developmentally inappropriate' and create a harmful 'hothouse' atmosphere.
claims.source_passage: According to the educational community, “research has shown” that untimely interventions and constraints are “development ally inappropriate” and create a hothouse, forced-feeding atmosphere.

claims.text: American parents have internalized a false dichotomy between joyful, nonacademic education and joyless, unnatural academic instruction.
claims.source_passage: Many parents have internalized the either-or polarity between joyful, nonacademic, “developmentally appropriate” education and joyless, unnatural academics.

claims.text: Challenging academic subject matter can be successfully combined with joyful, multisensory activities in early childhood education.
claims.source_passage: That challenging subject matter can be combined with joyful and concrete activities which engage all the senses is not imagined as a possibility.

claims.text: Children who attend academic preschools at a younger age are more academically effective and better adjusted than those who do not.
claims.source_passage: Those who attend school at a younger age are more effective academically and, by all indirect measures, better adjusted and happier for having had early exposure to challenging and stimulating early academic experiences.

claims.text: Academically focused preschools can overcome academic differences between social classes.
claims.source_passage: The French experience shows that a good, academically focused preschool can overcome the egregious academic differences that currently develop between social classes in American schools.

claims.text: Disadvantaged children who attend academic preschool at age two can reach academic parity with highly advantaged children by grade six or seven.
claims.source_passage: When disadvantaged children attend écoles maternelles at age two, their academic performance by grade six or seven equals that of highly advantaged children who have not attended preschool until age four.

claims.text: The poor results of Head Start are likely due to a lack of coherent content rather than the sociology of the students.
claims.source_passage: I suggested that the disappointing results of Head Start probably have more to do with the lack of a coherent approach to content (a hallmark of developmentalism) than with any unique problems in modern American society.

claims.text: Educational philosophy is the critical difference between the results of French preschools and American Head Start programs.
claims.source_passage: The contrast between the American and French results with Head Start-style programs amounts almost to a controlled experiment in which the critical differences between the programs lie more in the philosophy behind early schooling than in the sociology of the young pupils.

claims.text: Neurobiological research refutes the claim that early education must be characterized by extreme gradualness and hands-off spontaneity.
claims.source_passage: If one also brings in studies from neurobiology, the claim that early education requires extreme gradualness and hands-off spontaneity is refuted by research that has determined what “nature” is actually telling us about the brain’s development.

claims.text: Educational developmentalism is deeply culturally embedded in America, stemming from Emerson and Alcott.
claims.source_passage: Americans have breathed in developmentalism from the ambient air ever since the time of Emerson and Alcott.

claims.text: The professional version of developmentalism in American education is accepted without forceful argument because of long-standing Romantic notions about childhood in American culture.
claims.source_passage: Because of American culture’s long-standing Romantic notions about early childhood, the professional version of developmentalism does not have to be argued very forcefully.

claims.text: Research that contradicts developmentalist conclusions is typically ignored or dismissed within the educational community.
claims.source_passage: Research leading to a different conclusion is given short shrift. Studies by Harvard’s Jerome Bruner once led him to say that “any subject can be taught effectively in some intellectually honest form to any child at any stage of development.” This famous remark is usually quoted in order to be dismissed.

claims.text: Failure to train children's brains early through difficult tasks makes later learning significantly harder.
claims.source_passage: “The problem is, if you don’t train them early, it’s much harder.”

claims.text: Children's brains possess a unique ability to learn quickly between the ages of two and eleven due to an abundance of synaptic connections.
claims.source_passage: The greatest chiseling is accomplished between the ages of two and 11. ... in childhood there is an ability to learn quickly which is unparalleled.

claims.text: The educational guideline suggesting children should not master content that can be acquired 'more easily later' is factually incorrect and inefficient.
claims.source_passage: Children should not be expected to comprehend abstract/symbolic concepts or master skills or content that can be acquired more easily later on. ... It is doubtful that such a policy would contribute to “efficiency in teaching and learning.”

claims.text: Children in nations like France and Japan successfully master multi-digit arithmetic and place value by the end of first and second grade without strain.
claims.source_passage: In France and Japan and other developed nations, children at the end of first grade perform written operations with two-digit numbers and have an at least implicit understanding of place value to two places. ... all without great strain on them or their teachers and without any known harmful psychological effects.

claims.text: The difficulty American children face with math concepts like place value is caused by lack of instruction and practice, not developmental constraints.
claims.source_passage: The reported difficulty that American children under age ten have in understanding place value is very likely owing to their lack of consistent instruction and practice in arithmetic.

claims.text: Frequent manipulation of numbers leads from implicit understanding to explicit conceptual understanding of math principles.
claims.source_passage: For millions of children in Europe and Asia, constant familiarity with numbers and frequent manipulation of them bring first implicit and then, by second grade, explicit understanding of place value.

claims.text: The American educational 'thoughtworld' is characterized by a nearly total consensus among major professional organizations on developmentalist guidelines.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, the statement was supported by the following American professional associations: [list of 10 associations including ASCD, NCTM, IRA].

claims.text: Repetitive practice of procedural skills (meaningless tricks) is often the most effective path to conceptual understanding.
claims.source_passage: Yet, frequent practice in performing the “meaningless trick” is one way, perhaps the best way, to gain an operational and thence a conceptual understanding of place value.

claims.text: The process of learning place value in the early grades can be painless and even fun.
claims.source_passage: there is vivid evidence that the process is painless, and even fun.

claims.text: American professional associations advise deferring learning that is successfully taught in the first three grades in other countries.
claims.source_passage: To follow the advice given here would defer for several years learnings that, even in the United States, with its low scores in mathematics, are ostensibly scheduled to be taught in the first three grades.

claims.text: Educational questions should not be decided by the authority of professional organizations but by empirical principles.
claims.source_passage: The same empirical principle applies to the question of when it is best to teach place value.

claims.text: American professional education associations provide impractical and misleading advice regarding early-childhood instruction.
claims.source_passage: How did we arrive at a situation in which the American professional associations are setting forth what would be considered by most overseas experts and by mainstream American psychologists to be impractical and misleading advice?

claims.text: Romanticism's quasi-religious views of early childhood are more influential in the United States than anywhere else in the world, except perhaps Germany.
claims.source_passage: Romanticism’s quasi-religious views of early childhood took hold more firmly in the United States than anywhere else in the world, with the possible exception of Germany.

claims.text: Romantic doctrines about childhood initially took hold because they expressed partial truths.
claims.source_passage: Of course, one reason Romantic doctrines about childhood initially took hold was that they did express partial truths.

claims.text: The Romantic emphasis on the importance of play in early education is supported by both global practice and psychological research.
claims.source_passage: For instance, the Romantic emphasis on the importance of play in early education is accepted by educational systems throughout the world, as well as by mainstream psychological research.

claims.text: The practice of delaying book learning conflicts with effective research when it is accepted uncritically.
claims.source_passage: But other Romantic ideas, such as the importance of delaying book learning, when they are carried too far or accepted uncritically, conflict with effective practice and research.

claims.text: The doctrine of developmentalism (natural tempo) holds that bookish content should be delayed until after age eight.
claims.source_passage: the doctrine of developmentalism, or natural tempo, which holds that there is a natural age (usually after age eight) for introducing bookish content

claims.text: The doctrine of natural pedagogy holds that learning in a lifelike, holistic context is superior to lecture or drill-and-practice formats.
claims.source_passage: the doctrine of natural pedagogy, which holds that it is better to learn things naturally in a lifelike, thematic, holistic context than in a lecture or a drill-and-practice format.

claims.text: The doctrine of natural tempo is rooted in Romantic faith rather than scientific research.
claims.source_passage: But its ultimate source is Romantic faith in the holiness of natural growth. Such doctrine claims to be based on research, especially that of Piaget, but research does not confirm this.

claims.text: Jean Piaget did not support a purely naturalistic approach and believed environment could accelerate cognitive growth.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, Piaget himself (not to mention later researchers who have qualified his views) cautioned his uncritical adherents against a purely naturalistic approach

claims.text: Sound teaching methods can accelerate a student's cognitive growth without harming it.
claims.source_passage: that sound methods can therefore increase the students’ efficiency and even accelerate their spiritual growth without making it any the less sound.

claims.text: Jean Piaget cautioned against a purely naturalistic approach to education, acknowledging that environment plays a decisive role in mental development.
claims.source_passage: observed that environment can play a decisive role in the development of the mind; that the thought content of the stages and the ages at which they occur are not immutably fixed

claims.text: Sound educational methods can increase student efficiency and accelerate spiritual growth without compromising its soundness.
claims.source_passage: that sound methods can therefore increase the students’ efficiency and even accelerate their spiritual growth without making it any the less sound.

claims.text: The NAEYC guidelines advise deferring the teaching of place value in favor of meaningful conceptual organizers like projects.
claims.source_passage: the “Guidelines for Appropriate Curriculum Content and Assessment in Programs Serving Children Ages 3 Through 8,” which advise deferring place value: Conceptual organizers such as themes, units, or projects give children something meaningful and substantive to engage their minds.

claims.text: The superiority of a specific mode of instruction is an empirical question to be determined for specific subjects and groups of children, not a self-evident truth.
claims.source_passage: But whether such modes of instruction are always superior is a matter to be determined empirically for specific subjects and groups of children.

claims.text: A combination of 'showing' and 'telling' yields faster and more secure results in both writing and teaching than showing alone.
claims.source_passage: But a combination of showing and telling yields the fastest and securest results in both writing and teaching.

claims.text: For most subjects, a pedagogy that mixes indirect methods with direct, focused ones yields the highest student interest and attainment.
claims.source_passage: For most subjects, a pedagogy that mixes indirect, lifelike methods with direct, focused ones will yield the highest student interest and attainment.

claims.text: Specialized drill and practice is considered essential in teaching skills like ballet, piano, or downfield blocking.
claims.source_passage: teaching young children ballet, or piano playing, or downfield blocking, where some admixture of specialized drill and practice is thought to be essential.

claims.text: Natural pedagogy is an insecure way of learning foundational skills like grammar, spelling, phonics, and multiplication tables.
claims.source_passage: But it turns out to be a very insecure way of learning such things as standard grammar, spelling, phonics, and the multiplication table.

claims.text: Children who succeed in holistic schools often do so because they receive additional drill and practice in educated homes.
claims.source_passage: Most often the correct answer is that the children who do learn grammar, and phonics, and so on, come from educated homes where they receive additional drill and practice in those skills.

claims.text: The educational community's naturalistic premises prevent them from acknowledging the role of drill and practice in academic success.
claims.source_passage: But that commonsense answer is inconsistent with the premise of natural pedagogy, which spurns drill and practice (no matter where undertaken) as deadening and unnecessary.

claims.text: Natural pedagogy encourages a tendency to attribute academic achievement to innate talent or social determinism rather than focused work.
claims.source_passage: The doctrine of natural pedagogy thus encourages a tendency already strong in American culture—to connect academic achievement more with social determinism and natural talent than with focused hard work.

claims.text: Neurobiology indicates that all learning requires the effort of attention and often requires distributed practice to fix neural networks.
claims.source_passage: One finding of neurobiology is that all learnings require effort—first of all, the effort of attention; in addition, many learnings require repeated efforts (“distributed practice”) to forge and fix new neural networks.

claims.text: Secure learning will not occur unless educational efforts are directed and monitored by a teacher.
claims.source_passage: But unless they are directed and monitored—a primary responsibility of the teacher—secure learning will not occur.

claims.text: Many 'nonlearning' children are held back by educational neglect caused by undemanding naturalistic pedagogy rather than a lack of innate ability.
claims.source_passage: It is inconceivable to people who hold this point of view that these children have been held back by a history of educational neglect, caused by an undemanding naturalistic pedagogy.

claims.text: Effective learning of skills like the multiplication table does not occur through osmosis but requires effort.
claims.source_passage: One finding of neurobiology is that all learnings require effort—first of all, the effort of attention; in addition, many learnings require repeated efforts (“distributed practice”) to forge and fix new neural networks.

claims.text: The 'fallacy of natural pedagogy' incorrectly assumes that innate ability is the primary driver of learning success.
claims.source_passage: The fallacy of natural pedagogy thus breeds the further fallacy that innate ability is the royal road to all learning, that nonlearning children are being held back by lack of such ability rather than by lack of hard, directed work.

claims.text: Primary learnings follow a universal, transcultural sequence and are developmentally inherent.
claims.source_passage: These primary learnings (a concept introduced by David Geary) do seem to follow a definite and universal sequence, and therefore to support the Pestalozzi-Piaget model.

claims.text: Secondary learnings, such as reading, writing, and arithmetic, are non-natural and do not follow automatic developmental patterns.
claims.source_passage: But beyond these developmental universals, there are secondary learnings that, while necessary to particular cultures, are not universal. Among these nonnatural learnings are reading, writing, certain basic arithmetical operations... These secondary learnings do not fit the Pestalozzi-Piaget scheme of automatic development.

claims.text: Secondary learnings will not develop in a child unless they are specifically taught.
claims.source_passage: The learnings do not develop at all unless they are taught—a self-evident inference from their nonuniversality across cultures.

claims.text: There is no specific age at which a child becomes 'naturally' ready for reading, writing, or arithmetic.
claims.source_passage: the implication of research along these lines is that while... it is inefficient to teach certain aspects of reading, writing, and arithmetic too early... there is no age when a child is developmentally ready by nature for learning reading, writing, and arithmetic.

claims.text: The American early-childhood specialists' approach to readiness is excessively individualistic compared to successful international models.
claims.source_passage: That’s not a surprising conclusion, but it is less individualistic than the aforementioned “Position Statement” of American early-childhood specialists, which advises that “decisions about when knowledge and skills are introduced and/or expected to be accomplished are based on knowledge of prior experiences of individual children.”

claims.text: A child's readiness for secondary processes like reading is a product of prior relevant learning rather than biological development.
claims.source_passage: Children’s readiness for secondary processes such as reading and arithmetic is not simply a matter of natural development but also one of prior relevant learning. Learning builds on learning.

claims.text: Early childhood education should prioritize practical questions of time allocation and prior learning over quasi-religious concerns about 'spiritual damage.'
claims.source_passage: Thus the most urgent educational question in the crucial early years from three to seven is not a quasi-religious one concerning the inviolability of young childhood and the possibility of spiritual damage, but a practical question about what other learnings must be sacrificed in pursuing one kind of learning too intensively.

claims.text: After age six or seven, biological developmental constraints rarely limit the acquisition of secondary academic learnings.
claims.source_passage: But by the early school grades—that is, after age six or seven—the occasions where inherent slowness of primary development places powerful limitations on secondary learning are few.

claims.text: The educational question for children aged three to seven is a practical one regarding trade-offs in learning, rather than a quasi-religious concern about spiritual damage.
claims.source_passage: the crucial early years from three to seven is not a quasi-religious one concerning the inviolability of young childhood and the possibility of spiritual damage, but a practical question about what other learnings must be sacrificed in pursuing one kind of learning too intensively.

claims.text: There are no natural pathways for learning nonnatural, secondary processes like reading or mathematics.
claims.source_passage: While there is no natural way to learn nonnatural, secondary processes, many American experts would still insist on the Romantic doctrine that having a young child learn nonnatural processes through “drill and kill” or reward and punishment does psychological harm.

claims.text: Moderate stress and 'enticement' are beneficial rather than harmful in the early educative process.
claims.source_passage: Consistent pressure on young children is of course harmful and will backfire, but enticement and a little pressure are no bad thing.

claims.text: Effort and persistence are necessary requirements for almost all secondary learning.
claims.source_passage: Effort and persistence are needed for almost all secondary learning.

claims.text: Developmentalism has led to the American educational system repudiating the effort required for learning as 'unnatural.'
claims.source_passage: One effect has been to repudiate as unnatural the significant effort that all learning requires, whether it is painful or joyful.

claims.text: Successful educational systems encourage slower students to work more intensively rather than allowing them to progress at a 'natural' pace.
claims.source_passage: Yet a guiding principle of more successful educational systems holds that slower children should be encouraged to work all the longer and more intensively.

claims.text: Children can reach grade-level goals through extra effort without suffering harmful psychological effects.
claims.source_passage: Usually it is reached, but even when it is not, children approach it more closely by putting forth extra effort, and they do so without harmful psychological effect.

claims.text: Developmentalism’s assumption that learning speed is purely innate and genetically determined is often incorrect.
claims.source_passage: Extremely deleterious social consequences follow upon developmentalism’s overemphasis on natural (i.e., genetically determined) talent and its assumption that speed of learning is innate. The assumption is very often wrong.

claims.text: The correlation between academic performance and economic status in the United States is too high to be explained by innate biological talent alone.
claims.source_passage: In the United States, the all-too-consistent correlation between low academic performance and low economic status shows that something more than innate talent is determining the results.

claims.text: Developmentalism is a significant cause of educational inequity in the United States.
claims.source_passage: It is probable that developmentalism itself is a significant cause of the inequity.

claims.text: Developmentalism ensures that children from well-educated homes maintain an advantage because they bring background knowledge that the school fails to provide.
claims.source_passage: By encouraging an early education that is free of “unnatural” bookish knowledge... developmentalism virtually ensures that children from well-educated homes who happen to be primed with academically relevant background knowledge... will learn faster than disadvantaged children.

claims.text: Developmentalism withholds academic knowledge from students differentially based on their social class.
claims.source_passage: Developmentalism withholds academic knowledge on principle. But in the current social context it withholds it differentially, according to social class.

claims.text: The faster academic progress of advantaged students is caused by their greater intellectual capital rather than superior innate talent.
claims.source_passage: The home-provided background knowledge of advantaged students helps make them quicker and more academically advanced than their less-advantaged classmates—not because they have uniformly superior innate talent but because they have uniformly greater intellectual capital.

claims.text: Developmentalism ensures that children from well-educated homes learn faster than disadvantaged children by relying on the background knowledge those students bring from home.
claims.source_passage: developmentalism virtually ensures that children from well-educated homes who happen to be primed with academically relevant background knowledge, which they bring with them to school, will learn faster than disadvantaged children who do not bring such knowledge with them and do not receive it at school.

claims.text: Developmentalism withholds academic knowledge differentially according to a student's social class.
claims.source_passage: Developmentalism withholds academic knowledge on principle. But in the current social context it withholds it differentially, according to social class.

claims.text: The 'innate' pace of a child's development is difficult to ascertain and largely irrelevant to the speed at which American schoolchildren can actually learn.
claims.source_passage: the 'innate' pace of development that is 'natural' for each child would be very difficult to ascertain, and has little to do with the actual speed with which American schoolchildren are currently able to learn.

claims.text: Multiaged education is an inadequately tested practice derived from Romanticism rather than scientific evidence.
claims.source_passage: developmentalists have drawn upon the latent Romanticism in American culture to persuade state legislatures to mandate an unproved practice called 'multiaged education'

claims.text: The practical effect of multiaged education laws is to relieve schools and teachers of accountability for educational inequality.
claims.source_passage: But the practical effect of the law will be to make children, teachers, and schools unaccountable for improving the present inequitable state of affairs.

claims.text: Mandating multiage education without large-scale controlled testing is radical and unwise legislation.
claims.source_passage: To pass such legislation when the principle of multiage education has not been tested on a large scale, or tested at all with proper controls, is radical and unwise legislation

claims.text: American educators often dismiss international comparisons by citing American student diversity as a unique and insurmountable challenge.
claims.source_passage: international comparisons seem to many Americans irrelevant, inadequate, and even downright un-American. ... 'It’s all very well to talk about homogeneous countries like France or Korea, but what has that got to do with my school? I’ve got kids from seventeen nations.'

claims.text: Student diversity does not excuse or foreordain low academic achievement scores.
claims.source_passage: But those difficulties do not excuse or foreordain the low achievement scores of the children.

claims.text: Schools in the suburbs of Paris exhibit ethnic and social diversity comparable to that of major American cities like the Bronx and Miami.
claims.source_passage: With my own eyes I have seen schools in the suburbs of Paris where the ethnic and social diversity is equal to that of the Bronx and Miami.

claims.text: The American educational community has ignored the scientific findings of Stevenson and Stigler due to an 'invincibly complacent Americanism.'
claims.source_passage: this work of science has been left stranded on the shoals of the invincibly complacent Americanism of the educational community.

claims.text: Findings from comparative studies of Taiwan, Japan, and the United States reveal universal characteristics of successful elementary education systems.
claims.source_passage: The book’s findings are stunning and, reinforced by data from other countries, tell us a great deal about apparently universal characteristics of successful systems of elementary education.

claims.text: The Learning Gap by Stevenson and Stigler is potentially one of the most important books for American education published in the past fifty years.
claims.source_passage: It is potentially one of the most important books for American education published in the past fifty years.

claims.text: The data and analyses in The Learning Gap are scientifically beyond reproach and have not been challenged by scientists of standing.
claims.source_passage: From a scientific stand-point, the data and analyses are beyond reproach, and no scientist of standing has challenged them.

claims.text: Educational researchers who dismiss international comparisons often use pseudoarguments to preserve the intellectual comfort of their readers.
claims.source_passage: These nonrefereed “researchers” find ready publication in educational journals by preserving the intellectual comfort of their readers.

claims.text: The American educational system prioritizes values of egalitarianism, utilitarianism, and individualism over maximum academic achievement.
claims.source_passage: Our system, unlike theirs, has “distinctive characteristics which promote the American values of egalitarianism, utilitarianism, and individualism, which have given educational objectives other than maximum achievement higher priority than they have enjoyed in much of the rest of the industrialized world”

claims.text: American culture possesses a strong predisposition that finds it inappropriate to compare American institutions with those of other nations.
claims.source_passage: their comforting messages would be much less likely to be believed inside and outside the educational community if there were not throughout American culture a strong predisposition that finds it inappropriate to compare our institutions with those of other nations.

claims.text: American exceptionalism is a widespread set of attitudes claiming our country is so different from others that we have nothing to learn from them.
claims.source_passage: This is the sentiment historians call. “American exceptionalism”—a widespread set of attitudes that claim “distinctive characteristics” for our country and its institutions, making us so entirely different from other countries that we have little or nothing to learn from them, and ought not to be compared with them.

claims.text: American exceptionalism holds that the American people are better than other peoples and their problems are unique.
claims.source_passage: American exceptionalism holds that we as a people are not only different from but better than other peoples, and that our problems are unique.

claims.text: Historically, American schoolbooks used from 1790 to 1900 unanimously contrasted virtuous Americans with corrupt Europeans.
claims.source_passage: Ruth Elson, in her study of the books used in our schools from 1790 to 1900, showed that they unanimously contrasted virtuous and natural Americans with corrupt and decadent Europeans.

claims.text: The American Revolution contributed significantly to a pervasive sense of exceptionalism in popular culture.
claims.source_passage: The American Revolution contributed significantly to a strong sense of exceptionalism, one that became pervasive in our popular culture.

claims.text: American Romanticism includes an exceptionalist version that views Americans as freer, more independent, and more innocent than 'sinful' Europeans.
claims.source_passage: We of the “Virgin Land” were freer, more independent, more innocent, and more vigorously diverse than sinful Europe.

claims.text: The American sense of mission implies that the U.S. exists to teach other nations rather than learn from them.
claims.source_passage: We were destined to lead and redeem other nations, not vice versa. We were set on earth to teach them, not to learn from them.

claims.text: American Romanticism is defined by a belief in being free from the 'tainted past' and 'original sin,' which leads to a derogation of traditions and books.
claims.source_passage: The “stuff untainted” of our Western soil meant that we were free from the burden of the past—even free, by implication, from original sin. Freedom from a tainted past, celebration of the vast pure landscape, and a derogation of traditions and books—all were very American, very exceptionalist versions of Romanticism.

claims.text: The ideas that dominate the modern American educational community are a distorted version of John Dewey's ideas, formulated primarily by William Heard Kilpatrick.
claims.source_passage: historians have shown that Dewey’s name was given to a rather distorted version of his ideas by the direct leader of the new movement, William Heard Kilpatrick.

claims.text: American education requires an unfixed, dynamic approach because the nation is uniquely democratic, diverse, and dynamic compared to 'static' foreign systems.
claims.source_passage: The kind of education we needed, he argued, had to be as different from other nations’ as we ourselves were different... We, by contrast, were democratic, diverse, and dynamic. In his book Education for a Changing Civilization, Kilpatrick made a central theme out of an argument he had already made in his earlier Foundations of Method—that America required an unfixed, dynamic approach as befitted our unique nation

claims.text: In a rapidly changing world, the curriculum should focus on thinking and 'methods of attack' rather than set subject matter.
claims.source_passage: The subject matter of the American curriculum could never be set in advance: “If people face a rapidly shifting and changing world, changing in unexpected ways and in unexpected directions, then what? Why, their education would stress thinking and methods of attack.”

claims.text: Modern educational rhetoric about the 'Information Age' is a re-branded version of 1920s Kilpatrickism.
claims.source_passage: Now we must avoid fixed and static content in order to enter the information age. Kilpatrickism is brought up to date by saying that what he called the “rapidly shifting and changing world... has been caused not by the industrial but by the computer revolution. But the goal which that new condition implies for American education is still said to be what Kilpatrick said it was in 1925: to ignore content (which is quickly outmoded) and to “stress thinking and methods of attack.”

claims.text: Post-WWII British educational progressivism was heavily influenced by and reinforced American educational theories.
claims.source_passage: Until recently, the British educational community was dominated by anti-subject-matter progressivism almost as vigorously as ours has been, particularly after World War II, when all things American were held in high regard. The two educational communities reinforced each other.

claims.text: Continental nations like France and Sweden achieved better educational results than Britain due to a coherent emphasis on content.
claims.source_passage: it became widely known to the British public that France, Sweden, and other nations on the Continent were achieving much better educational results than Britain—results clearly associated with a more coherent emphasis on content.

claims.text: The British public supported the introduction of a national curriculum because they witnessed the superior performance of systems using national curricula.
claims.source_passage: This direct knowledge gradually led British public opinion to support the idea of grade-by-grade school content. By 1987, it had become crystal clear to a majority of Britons that school systems which used national curricula were performing much better than the British school system.

claims.text: Moderating American exceptionalism in the educational sphere is a high priority for achieving educational improvement in the United States.
claims.source_passage: Moderating our American exceptionalism in the educational sphere is going to be a high priority for achieving educational improvement in the United States.

claims.text: Britain introduced a national curriculum because they were less isolated from external influences and gained firsthand acquaintance with better-performing international school systems.
claims.source_passage: Being less isolated from external influences than we in the United States, and perhaps less afflicted by so extreme a sense of national uniqueness, they gained firsthand acquaintance with other school systems. This direct knowledge gradually led British public opinion to support the idea of grade-by-grade school content.

claims.text: American educational localism is rooted in a historical rejection of central authority, originally to protect against despotism and later to resist bureaucratic mindlessness.
claims.source_passage: Protection from despotic control was the primary idea of localism in the early years. Later on, it was extended to resist another kind of tyranny—that of bureaucratic mindlessness, indifference, and inefficiency.

claims.text: The United States has historically abandoned local control in favor of national standards in domains like currency, railroads, and time zones when practicalities required change.
claims.source_passage: We have chosen to abandon local currencies printed by individual banks in favor of a national currency. We have adopted standard-gauge railroads. We set our watches by translocal time zones rather than by local sun time.

claims.text: Educational quality and equity require a measure of translocal commonality in the content of early schooling.
claims.source_passage: In most nations of the modern world, people have also recognized that educational quality and equity require a measure of translocal commonality in the content of early schooling.

claims.text: Local control of education must be taken as a starting point for American policy because there is no plausible mechanism for replacing it.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, it is quixotic to resist educational localism in the United States, where there is no plausible mechanism for replacing this sanctified arrangement. Hence, local control of education must be taken as a starting point for policy.

claims.text: Localism in education is not a protection against centralized thought-control; rather, it leads to unfairness by failing to ensure quality education for all children.
claims.source_passage: The Japanese replied in effect, “We intend to follow your principles of government, but localism in education is not a protection against anything. It mainly leads to unfairness to students, because it does not provide any means for insuring that all children receive a quality education.”

claims.text: Extreme population mobility in the United States makes the tempering of educational localism imperative.
claims.source_passage: The extreme mobility figures in the United States, cited in Chapter 2, make a tempering of our localism even more imperative.

claims.text: The argument that common content infringes on teacher autonomy is a false 'canard' that has been promulgated by education schools since the 1920s.
claims.source_passage: a canard that has been promulgated by education schools since William Heard Kilpatrick enunciated it in the 1920s.

claims.text: Content commonality does not diminish teacher prestige; rather, it exists in nations where teachers have the most prestige and highest salaries.
claims.source_passage: Proof of the irrelevance of content commonality to professional status is that it exists in just those nations where teachers have the most prestige and are paid the highest salaries.

claims.text: Extreme localism is a formula for failure and inequity in education.
claims.source_passage: Extreme localism has proved itself to be a formula for failure and inequity.

claims.text: Refusing to make curricular decisions or give curricular advice is an abandonment of the fundamental responsibilities of educational leadership.
claims.source_passage: But this is to abandon fundamental responsibilities of educational leadership. Always leaving content up to somebody else is a pedagogical and political irresponsibility, not a democratic virtue.

claims.text: Teachers need agreed-on goals and cooperation with colleagues to do their jobs properly.
claims.source_passage: Extreme localism has failed not only American children but also the teachers themselves, who, to do their job properly, need agreed-on goals and the benefit of cooperation with their colleagues.

claims.text: Content commonality in a school system is correlated with high teacher prestige and salaries.
claims.source_passage: content commonality to professional status is that it exists in just those nations where teachers have the most prestige and are paid the highest salaries.

claims.text: Common curricular goals provide teachers with more classroom freedom and better opportunities for professional cooperation.
claims.source_passage: These teachers enjoy a great deal of freedom in the classroom, as well as the benefit of cooperation with their colleagues, based on the common goals they share.

claims.text: Leaving curriculum content entirely to local discretion is a pedagogical and political irresponsibility.
claims.source_passage: Always leaving content up to somebody else is a pedagogical and political irresponsibility, not a democratic virtue.

claims.text: Independent-mindedness was successfully sustained in American schools in the nineteenth century when the curriculum focused on subject matter.
claims.source_passage: Independent-mindedness used to be sustained just as diligently in the nineteenth century, when our schools stressed subject matter, as it is now, when they stress “critical thinking.”

claims.text: It is a fallacy to believe that independent-mindedness can be sustained by disparaging factual knowledge.
claims.source_passage: It is a fallacy to believe that independent-mindedness can be sustained by disparaging factual knowledge.

claims.text: Effective independent thinking requires a thorough mastery of the received views one intends to challenge.
claims.source_passage: there has never been an effective independent thinker who did not thoroughly master the received views against which he or she rebeled.

claims.text: American exceptionalism prevents the public from accepting educational changes based on rational or pragmatic principles.
claims.source_passage: The danger created by American exceptionalism is that until its irrational complacencies are neutralized, it may be impossible to introduce educational change based on pragmatic or rational principles.

claims.text: The belief in American exceptionalism is used to justify 'break-the-mold' experiments while ignoring international evidence of what works in education.
claims.source_passage: To a public indoctrinated with exceptionalism, it does not seem odd that typical proposals for educational improvement should be based on homegrown American “break-the-mold” experiments that have never been tried on a large scale—as if it were irrelevant that there already exists a body of knowledge regarding what does and does not work elsewhere.

claims.text: Modern American educational individualism is a late arrival influenced by European Romanticism and differs from pre-1850 American ideas.
claims.source_passage: Our present-day emphasis on the absolute worth and uniqueness of each child came to the American scene late, arriving only after European Romanticism had been transported to New England, whence it emerged victorious in the nineteenth century.

claims.text: Educational rhetoric about 'individual differences' is a technical mask for the Romanticism of Pestalozzi and Froebel.
claims.source_passage: The scientific and social terms with which we celebrate “individual differences” are mainly technical masks for the robust Romanticism of Pestalozzi and Froebel.

claims.text: The phenomenon of grade inflation in schools is a consequence of Romantic individualism.
claims.source_passage: The school phenomenon that is currently called ‐grade inflation‑ is a perhaps inevitable consequence of Romantic individualism.

claims.text: The modern concept of 'genius' in education incorrectly suggests that natural instinct can replace the need for learned rules and structured practice.
claims.source_passage: Genius can set us right in composition without the rules of the learned.

claims.text: The belief that each child possesses a special 'genius' is a sentiment derived from 18th-century Romantic thought, specifically Edward Young.
claims.source_passage: He probably did not realize that his sentiment descended from the father of all grade inflation, Edward Young, who said as early as 1759 that all writers have innate genius.

claims.text: Academic competition is discouraged in American schools because it is seen as subverting the egalitarian and individualist creed.
claims.source_passage: Since in American schools every child is unique and of equal worth with every other child, academic competition, which subverts this egalitarian and individualist creed, must be discouraged.

claims.text: Grade inflation is more accurately described as 'grade egalitarianism' or 'grade individualism.'
claims.source_passage: What has been called ‐grade inflation‑ is perhaps more accurately called ‐grade egalitarianism‑ or ‐grade individualism.‑

claims.text: High academic self-esteem has little empirical correlation with actual academic accomplishment.
claims.source_passage: Dawes has shown the lack of correlation (.17) between academic self-esteem and academic accomplishment.

claims.text: The only consistent correlative of academic achievement is effort.
claims.source_passage: While self-contempt may indeed lead to idleness, the only consistent correlative of academic achievement is effort, which is not well correlated with self-esteem.

claims.text: The self-esteem movement in education contains unspoken and condescending racial implications.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, the compensatory, racial overtones of the self-esteem movement should be exposed for what they are. For beneath the brave words about individualism, self-esteem, and intrinsic worth, there are often unspoken racial implications in the practice of compensatory grading.

claims.text: Equalizing grades has failed to equalize the actual academic achievements of different social, ethnic, and racial groups.
claims.source_passage: Although grades may have become more equal, schools have made little or no progress in equalizing the actual academic achievements of social, ethnic, and racial groups.

claims.text: Schools and colleges attempt to abolish the achievement gap by fiat, either by interpreting test scores differentially by race or rejecting test validity.
claims.source_passage: This latter tactic, the achieving of equality by fiat, is accomplished at schools and colleges by interpreting standardized test scores differentially according to race and social class, or by simply rejecting the validity of the tests and the ‐false‑ message that their scores send.

claims.text: The College Board's 'nonlinear transformation' of SAT scores was an artificial raising of grades to mask a failure to raise achievement.
claims.source_passage: The salvaging of individual worth and self-esteem by artificially raising grades when schooling has failed to raise achievement was exemplified in the recent decision by the College Board simply to lift SAT scores.

claims.text: Artificial score inflation by the College Board invites complacency and masks a racially condescending determinism.
claims.source_passage: Instead of keeping the existing standard in place to stimulate students and schools to greater effort, the College Board action invites complacency about the achievements of students and of American education.

claims.text: Passing students by fiat constitutes a 'therapy by illusion' that merely delays an inevitable time of reckoning.
claims.source_passage: Passing students by fiat is therapy by illusion. ... But the therapy of illusion simply delays the time of reckoning.

claims.text: American schools teach students to blame external society rather than the failures of the school system for their lack of success.
claims.source_passage: Meanwhile, they have been taught by the schools not to blame the schools (which seem not to grasp the close affinity between condescension and racism) but to blame instead the society beyond the schools for failing to give them a fair shake.

claims.text: The educational community practices a form of 'illusionist therapy' upon itself to preserve its own self-esteem.
claims.source_passage: had turned the illusionist therapy the educational community practices on students upon itself in order to preserve its own self-esteem.

claims.text: The American education world relies heavily on the rhetoric of Romantic individualism but lacks practical ideas and hope for student success.
claims.source_passage: The actions of the College Board and the self-esteem movement compellingly reveal that the education world is long on the rhetoric of Romantic individualism but short on ideas and hope.

claims.text: Students praised for innate excellence rather than achievement later struggle to maintain employment.
claims.source_passage: Students who have been praised for their innate excellence and unique inner worth later find that they cannot hold down good jobs.

claims.text: Introducing multicultural elements into the early curriculum is useful when it recognizes the real achievements and contributions of minorities.
claims.source_passage: When done well, introducing multicultural elements into the early curriculum has been useful to American children because it has recognized real achievements and contributions by minorities.

claims.text: Multiculturalism in the curriculum is insufficient by itself to significantly impact student achievement levels.
claims.source_passage: Although continued educational inequity in schools where multi-culturalism has been introduced demonstrates that this curricular shift is not sufficient by itself to make much difference in achievement levels

claims.text: The primary cause for the difference in educational attainment between West Indian blacks and U.S. blacks is cultural rather than individual or genetic.
claims.source_passage: Patterson’s analysis makes it overwhelmingly clear that the chief explanation must be cultural rather than individual and genetic.

claims.text: Academic success depends more on the attitudes of parents, students, and teachers toward the dominant culture than on material resources.
claims.source_passage: School success does not seem to depend on the physical condition of the home or of the school; it is more profoundly related to attitudes towards the dominant culture on the part of the parents, the students, and the teachers.

claims.text: Condescending attempts to induce self-esteem through praise or grading are harmful because children recognize the falsehood.
claims.source_passage: These amateur psychological efforts fail because they lie to children about their achievements. The falsehood and the implicit condescension are ultimately picked up by children, who, for all their enforced ignorance, are natively smart.

claims.text: The only reliable method for building student self-confidence is nurturing the hard work that leads to real academic achievement.
claims.source_passage: The only sure method of making children self-confident about their work in schools (which will help their general self-esteem) is, as Patterson and Dawes suggest, to nurture the hard work that will lead to actual academic achievement.

claims.text: Efforts to help minorities through multicultural curricular elements may be socially and psychologically useful.
claims.source_passage: Some of the efforts to help minorities, such as the introduction of multicultural curricular elements, may be socially and psychologically useful

claims.text: Amateur psychological efforts in schools fail because they lie to children about their actual achievements.
claims.source_passage: These amateur psychological efforts fail because they lie to children about their achievements.

claims.text: Children possess a native intelligence that allows them to detect falsehood and implicit condescension in school praise.
claims.source_passage: The falsehood and the implicit condescension are ultimately picked up by children, who, for all their enforced ignorance, are natively smart.

claims.text: Skepticism caused by false praise leads to a further erosion of a child's self-esteem.
claims.source_passage: Their resulting skepticism leads to further erosion of their self-esteem.

claims.text: The 'individual learning styles' and 'multiple intelligences' movements are modern, scientized versions of Romantic individualism.
claims.source_passage: In recent years, a new ‘scientific’ validation of Romantic individualism has generated a lot of interest in the schools—the ‘individual learning styles’ movement, together with its most recent variant, the ‘multiple intelligences’ movement.

claims.text: The principle of compensatory intelligence suggests that every person is innately smart in at least one of seven types of intelligence.
claims.source_passage: The basic principle is that of compensatory intelligence, which makes everybody smart in something.

claims.text: The educational community consistently elevates ideologically pleasing, non-consensus scientific findings over ideologically troublesome consensus science.
claims.source_passage: The enthusiasm for the doctrines of individual learning styles and multiple intelligences... exemplifies the consistent and troublesome tendency of the educational community to elevate ideologically pleasing but nonconsensus scientific findings over ideologically troublesome ones that have achieved scientific consensus.

claims.text: Adopting non-consensus science as the basis for school policy is a recipe for continued practical failure.
claims.source_passage: This is a recipe for continued practical failure in our schools.

claims.text: Scientific consensus guarantees a high degree of reliability for practical activities like schooling.
claims.source_passage: As consensus in science increases, so does the likelihood of correctness... even in those rare cases where it is slightly wrong, it reflects highly consistent practical success.

claims.text: Using unproven scientific theories in schools constitutes perilous human experimentation on a large scale.
claims.source_passage: To adopt nonconsensus science as the basis of school policy is to conduct very perilous human experimentation on a large scale without a license and with little hope of practical success.

claims.text: Neither multiple intelligences theory nor multiple learning styles theory is well accepted in the scientific community.
claims.source_passage: But neither the multiple intelligences theory nor the similar multiple learning styles theory is well accepted in the scientific community.

claims.text: Without an explanatory theory, the chance of correctly categorizing or measuring intelligence is nearly zero.
claims.source_passage: In the absence of an explanatory theory, the chance of accidentally making the right observations—describing the right phenomena, creating the right categories, making the right measurements—is small to the vanishing point.

claims.text: The educational utility of identifying multiple intelligence profiles is limited because there is no consensus on whether instruction should play to a student's strengths or bolster their weaknesses.
claims.source_passage: hut since nobody knows whether the educator should play to the student’s strengths or bolster the student’s weaknesses (or both), the new psychometrics does not seem to advance practical matters much beyond present psychometrics.

claims.text: Howard Gardner's catalogue of multiple intelligences is wrong.
claims.source_passage: Gardner’s catalogue of intelligences is wrong.

claims.text: In the absence of an explanatory theory, the chance of making accurate observations or creating valid categories in psychometrics is vanishingly small.
claims.source_passage: In the absence of an explanatory theory, the chance of accidentally making the right observations—describing the right phenomena, creating the right categories, making the right measurements—is small to the vanishing point.

claims.text: Current discussions about replacing single-value intelligence assessments with multiple intelligence profiles are based on hunch and opinion rather than established psychometrics.
claims.source_passage: Since none of the work has been done that would have to he done before a single-value assessment of intelligence could be replaced by a seven-value assessment, the discussion is all hunch and opinion.

claims.text: Educational research cannot determine whether schools should prioritize a student's special talents over standard academic competencies.
claims.source_passage: Should schools develop a student’s special talent and style of learning at the expense of developing standard academic competencies such as reading, speaking, mathematics, and general knowledge? Research cannot answer that question.
