claims.text: Educational equity requires that schools provide all children with the specific knowledge and skills needed for political, economic, and autonomous functioning.
claims.source_passage: Equity, however, clearly requires that schools give all children the knowledge and skills they need to become politically functional, economically successful, and autonomous citizens.

claims.text: Unless schools define specific attainments and ensure all students reach them, students cannot develop their potential as participants in the larger society.
claims.source_passage: If schools do not define with some particularity what those attainments are, and if they do not cause every student to reach them, no amount of overt concern for individuality can enable each student to develop his or her potential as a participant in the larger society.

claims.text: American professors of education are uniquely hostile to the teaching of factual knowledge compared to their peers in other nations.
claims.source_passage: That American professors of education are more hostile to the teaching of factual knowledge than education professors elsewhere in the world offers another point of entry into the American educational Thoughtworld.

claims.text: Opposition to factual knowledge standards in the United States unites diverse groups, including teachers, parents, and politicians from both major parties.
claims.source_passage: But, as the report from the Washington Post indicates, it is not just education professors who express hostility to “rote memorization.” That attitude also rallies Republicans and Democrats, parents and legislators, and, as I infer from the tenor of the Post article, newspaper reporters as well.

claims.text: Richard Hofstadter’s definition of anti-intellectualism as contempt for 'knowledge for its own sake' fails to capture the specific American scorn for academic and book-based knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Hofstadter defines anti-intellectualism as contempt for “knowledge for its own sake.” This definition perhaps misses something essential, namely, that the knowledge most often scorned by Americans tends to be academic knowledge connected with scientific lore and past traditions—the kind taught in lecture halls and recorded mostly in books.

claims.text: Americans traditionally value knowledge derived directly from experience more than knowledge derived from books.
claims.source_passage: Befitting our early image of ourselves as giving mankind a new, Edenic start in history, Americans have valued knowledge that comes directly from experience more than knowledge that comes from books.

claims.text: The American preference for 'critical thinking' over 'rote memorization' is a manifestation of the preference for direct experience over the recorded writings of others.
claims.source_passage: “Critical thinking” about one’s own direct experience is to be preferred to “rote memorization” of the writings of others.

claims.text: American thought has been conflicted regarding the value of book learning and scholarship since the mid-nineteenth century.
claims.source_passage: American thought has been conflicted about the value of book learning since the mid-nineteenth century.

claims.text: Despite anti-intellectual trends, Americans have maintained a faith in the social and economic utility of universal education and scholarship derived from the Enlightenment.
claims.source_passage: With one part of our minds we have remained loyal to the Jeffersonian and Enlightenment faith in the utility of scholarship, research, and ever-advancing knowledge.

claims.text: The Enlightenment tradition in America views the diffusion of knowledge as essential for democratic social and political efficacy.
claims.source_passage: With one part of our minds we have remained loyal to the Jeffersonian and Enlightenment faith in the utility of scholarship, research, and ever-advancing knowledge.

claims.text: The Romantic tradition in America disparages books and formal culture as barriers to individual divinity and practical experience.
claims.source_passage: Thus arose a conflict between our Enlightenment tradition, which connected democracy with book learning, and our Romantic tradition, which disparaged culture and books.

claims.text: Ralph Waldo Emerson viewed formal schooling as a hindrance to physical competency and a producer of 'a belly full of words' without true understanding.
claims.source_passage: We are shut up in schools and college recitation rooms for ten or fifteen years & come out at last with a belly full of words & do not know a thing. We cannot use our hands or our legs or our eyes or our arms.

claims.text: The contemporary educationist preference for 'hands-on' knowledge over verbal knowledge is a direct ideological descendant of 1830s Emersonian thought.
claims.source_passage: There is a direct line of descent from Emerson’s antibookish writings of the 1830s to the insistence on practical skills in the Cardinal Principles of 1918.

claims.text: By 1918, anti-academic sentiment had become the dominant principle taught in American education schools.
claims.source_passage: By that date, antiacademic sentiment, while simply an element of general American thought, had become the dominant principle taught in education schools.

claims.text: The 1918 Cardinal Principles report successfully shifted the focus of American schooling toward nonacademic goals like health, vocation, and leisure.
claims.source_passage: The focus in Cardinal Principles on such nonacademic goals as health, vocation, citizenship, worthy use of leisure, and the like has remained stable through various permutations of educational vocabulary from 1918 to the present.

claims.text: Progressive educators like William Heard Kilpatrick advocated for the abandonment of separate school subjects in favor of 'purposeful activity.'
claims.source_passage: The separate school subjects. Shall we not have to give them up if the ideas of purposeful activity and intrinsic subject-matter be adopted? As hitherto conceived and taught, yes; separate subjects for children would have to go.

claims.text: Vocational and practical training were explicitly deemed superior to traditional academic disciplines like geometry, French, and literature by influential reformers.
claims.source_passage: In 1939, Charles Prosser, an influential opponent of bookishness, stated in parallel clauses... that business arithmetic is superior to plane or solid geometry; learning ways of keeping physically fit, to the study of French; learning the technique of selecting an occupation, to the study of algebra.

claims.text: Educators should be concerned primarily with the growth of children and only secondarily with the learning of subject matter.
claims.source_passage: We are properly concerned first with children that they shall grow, and only secondarily with subject matter that it he learned.

claims.text: Separate school subjects should be abandoned in favor of purposeful activity and intrinsic subject matter.
claims.source_passage: The separate school subjects. Shall we not have to give them up if the ideas of purposeful activity and intrinsic subject-matter be adopted? As hitherto conceived and taught, yes; separate subjects for children would have to go.

claims.text: Utilitarian vocational skills, such as business arithmetic or physical fitness, are superior to traditional academic subjects like geometry or French.
claims.source_passage: business arithmetic is superior to plane or solid geometry; learning ways of keeping physically fit, to the study of French; learning the technique of selecting an occupation, to the study of algebra

claims.text: There is no inherent hierarchy or 'aristocracy' of academic subjects over practical or vocational ones.
claims.source_passage: there is no aristocracy of subjects.… Mathematics and mechanics, art and agriculture, history and homemaking are all peers.

claims.text: The leveling of educational hierarchies resulted in the suppression of traditional academic subjects in favor of nonacademic ones like home economics and personal adjustment.
claims.source_passage: In practice, this leveling of educational hierarchies resulted in the suppression of traditional subject categories in favor of nontraditional, nonacademic ones like home economics, consumer practices, and personal adjustment.

claims.text: By the 1940s, utilitarian educational practices that centered the curriculum around basic areas of human activity rather than traditional subjects became widespread in public schools.
claims.source_passage: By the 1940s, according to Diane Ravitch, these utilitarian ideas and practices had become widespread in public schools: Their common features were: centering the curriculum around basic areas of human activity, instead of traditional subject matter

claims.text: Anti-intellectual educational traditions are no longer viable or useful in the contemporary world.
claims.source_passage: These anti-intellectual traditions have not proved viable or useful in the contemporary world.

claims.text: In a shifting job market, traditional academic subjects like algebra are more practical than specific vocational training.
claims.source_passage: With the nature of jobs shifting every few years, it has become obvious that algebra is in fact the more practical study.

claims.text: A general ability to learn, predicated on broad general knowledge and vocabulary, is a more practical tool for the modern economy than direct vocational training.
claims.source_passage: Hence, a general ability to learn, based on broad general knowledge and vocabulary, is a more practical tool than direct vocational training.

claims.text: The Romantic prejudice against 'verbal' learning has no firm connection to reality and has caused fateful consequences for American education.
claims.source_passage: Under these circumstances, the Romantic attack on the “merely” verbal has had fateful consequences for modern American education. ... The prejudice against the verbal is itself a cultural and verbal tradition that has no firm connection to reality.

claims.text: Every decent job in a modern economy depends upon communication and learning facilitated through words.
claims.source_passage: Every decent job available in a modern economy is dependent upon communication and learning—two activities that take place primarily through the medium of words.

claims.text: Words are indispensable human tools for understanding realities, not just abstract symbols.
claims.source_passage: Verbal understanding is not “merely” verbal. Words are the indispensable human tools for understanding realities.

claims.text: Gaining a broad knowledge of words constitutes gaining a knowledge of the things to which those words refer.
claims.source_passage: Gaining a broad knowledge of words, and therefore also a knowledge of the things to which words refer, must be among the most practical and significant educational goals in our time.

claims.text: Learning words is an active trial-and-error process of hypothesis-making, not a form of rote memorization.
claims.source_passage: We don’t think of a child’s learning of words as “rote memorization.” ... As psychologists have shown, learning words requires complex trial-and-error guesses.

claims.text: Universally effective whole-class instruction is impossible when students have vastly different levels of academic preparation and vocabulary knowledge.
claims.source_passage: When classes are exceptionally heterogeneous in academic preparation and knowledge of words, as they often are in the United States, universally effective whole-class instruction is impossible.

claims.text: Disadvantaged children can only catch up in vocabulary through the direct targeting of specific words to be learned.
claims.source_passage: Sometimes, therefore, as Isabel Beck has shown, disadvantaged children can only catch up in their vocabulary by a direct targeting of the words that need to be learned.

claims.text: Communication requires a bare minimum of relevant background knowledge to be successful.
claims.source_passage: But sometimes when a child does not know enough context, he or she cannot at that moment be an active participant in the class, no matter how resourceful the child or teacher may be.

claims.text: The meanings of words are essentially brute facts that must be learned by rote because there is no logical connection between a word and the thing it identifies.
claims.source_passage: But whether a word is learned by targeted practice or by the contextual method of enriched language use, its actual meaning is, for the most part, just a brute fact. In a sense, all words are learned by rote.

claims.text: Daily recitation of seemingly meaningless texts, such as the Pledge of Allegiance, encourages trial-and-error inferences that eventually lead to conceptual understanding.
claims.source_passage: Such an apparently mindless daily performance as making first graders recite the (to them) meaningless sounds of the Pledge of Allegiance encourages trial-and-error inferences that result in many children understanding the meaning of the Pledge by fifth grade.

claims.text: Rote memorization of difficult scientific or geographical terms can serve as a means of gaining eventual understanding and critical thought.
claims.source_passage: He conceded the great power of verbal rote memorization as a means of gaining understanding and critical thought

claims.text: Early Romantic educational innovations were successful because they maintained definite knowledge goals and mixed traditional practices with new methods.
claims.source_passage: But one reason that the early Romantic innovations in education—from Pestalozzi to Colonel Parker’s school in Quincy, Massachusetts, or Dewey’s Lab School in Chicago—were so successful was that these early versions of progressivism retained definite knowledge goals and mixed traditional with untraditional practices.

claims.text: A systematic mastery of vocabulary provides children with a cognitive framework to fix objects in their consciousness and understand the relations between things.
claims.source_passage: Familiarity with the name enables them to fix the object whenever it enters their consciousness, and a logical and correct series of names develops and maintains in them a consciousness of the vital relation of things to each other.

claims.text: To achieve social justice, schools must directly teach all children the specific knowledge that children from privileged homes acquire indirectly at home.
claims.source_passage: in order to enhance the knowledge of those who come from underprivileged homes, it is necessary to teach all students in a focused and direct way the knowledge which the children of privilege gain indirectly by constant exposure and repetition at home.

claims.text: The developmentalist approach to schooling is inherently unfair because it falsely assumes that withholding academic knowledge will produce equal results for children of different social classes.
claims.source_passage: The unfairness of an antibookish or developmentalist approach to schooling lies in its assumption that knowledge can be equally withheld from the children of merchants and the children of peasants to achieve the same results.

claims.text: To enhance the knowledge of underprivileged children, schools must provide the focused and direct instruction of knowledge that privileged children acquire indirectly at home.
claims.source_passage: in order to enhance the knowledge of those who come from underprivileged homes, it is necessary to teach all students in a focused and direct way the knowledge which the children of privilege gain indirectly by constant exposure and repetition at home.

claims.text: Antibookish or developmentalist approaches to schooling are unfair because they assume knowledge can be withheld from all children without affecting results.
claims.source_passage: The unfairness of an antibookish or developmentalist approach to schooling lies in its assumption that knowledge can be equally withheld from the children of merchants and the children of peasants to achieve the same results.

claims.text: Pestalozzi utilized systematic instruction and direct teaching in the service of social justice.
claims.source_passage: Pestalozzi was flexible enough to use common sense in the service of social justice.

claims.text: Ralph Waldo Emerson’s educational anti-intellectualism was elitist, whereas Horace Mann’s approach was democratic.
claims.source_passage: Emerson was the elitist, Horace Mann the true democrat.

claims.text: Romantic anti-intellectualism and developmentalism are luxuries that the poor and the contemporary middle class cannot afford.
claims.source_passage: Romantic anti-intellectualism and developmentalism, as Gramsci understood, are luxuries of the merchant class that the poor cannot afford. For that matter, neither can the contemporary middle class in the United States.

claims.text: The Enlightenment view—that systematic knowledge is superior to unlettered experience—is the only viable approach for modern society.
claims.source_passage: Today, the Enlightenment view of the value of knowledge is the only view we can afford... they insisted upon the superior practicality of books over unlettered experience, and the greater utility of knowledge over ignorance.

claims.text: The theory of 'mental discipline' (that hard subjects generally train the mind) was scientifically disproved by Edward Lee Thorndike.
claims.source_passage: educationist opponents of Latin and other traditional subjects, using the research of Edward Lee Thorndike as a battering ram, rejected mental discipline as scientifically disproved.

claims.text: Cognitive skills are not transferred from one domain to another.
claims.source_passage: Thorndike had shown that skills are not transferred from one domain to another. Learning Latin did not 'teach you to think,' it just taught you Latin.

claims.text: Modern faith in general 'critical-thinking' skills lacks scientific justification.
claims.source_passage: Today, anti-subject-matter educationists profess faith in general, 'critical-thinking' skills much as traditionalists did, but with just as little scientific justification.

claims.text: The focus on 'practical know-how' and anti-subject-matter schooling has widened the academic and economic gap between haves and have-nots.
claims.source_passage: But what in fact occurred and still occurs is a widening of the academic and economic gap between haves and have-nots.

claims.text: Teaching 'practical' antibookish skills like critical thinking is both impractical and inequitable.
claims.source_passage: Teaching 'practical,' antibookish skills such as critical thinking has turned out to be highly impractical and inequitable.

claims.text: Inner-city children possess strong critical-thinking skills in contexts relevant to their experience, proving that the skills themselves are not the deficit.
claims.source_passage: Street-smart children can think very critically in situations that would stump their suburban peers. If their critical-thinking skills could be massively transferred from the street to the classroom, special training in this area would be quite unnecessary.

claims.text: The failure of the critical-thinking movement to close the academic gap is evidence that it lacks a basis in reality.
claims.source_passage: The incapacity of the critical-thinking movement to make a dent in the academic gap between haves and have-nots is strong evidence that this version of anti-intellectualism lacks a basis in reality.

claims.text: Education professors in research universities suffer from chronic status deprivation and low esteem among their colleagues.
claims.source_passage: professors of education in these institutions are held in low esteem as a group by their colleagues. Even their defenders... speak of 'their multifaceted and chronic status deprivation.'

claims.text: The guiding ideas and attitudes at teacher-training schools throughout the nation originate in the education schools of research universities.
claims.source_passage: the guiding ideas and attitudes at teacher-training schools throughout the nation have originated in the education schools of research universities.

claims.text: Professors of education in research universities are held in low esteem by their colleagues in other departments.
claims.source_passage: Both critics and defenders of education schools agree that professors of education in these institutions are held in low esteem as a group by their colleagues.

claims.text: Education schools have historically prioritized instructional technique over the mastery of important curricular knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In 1916, Abraham Flexner wrote that education schools had “lost their heads” when they called for the removal of important knowledge as well as trivia from the school curriculum. He complained of their unwarranted devotion to technique.

claims.text: Education schools exercise dominant influence in teacher certification and national school ideology despite their low status within universities.
claims.source_passage: the plight of education schools in the universities is counterbalanced by their enormous importance in the sphere of teacher certification and by their huge ideological influence in the nation’s schools.

claims.text: The educational community's characterization of knowledge as 'elitism' is a psychological defense mechanism born of professional resentment.
claims.source_passage: The educational community’s identification of knowledge with “elitism”—a theme that long antedated the recent addition of “Eurocentrism” to the antiknowledge armory—is a strategy born more of hostility than of rational principle.

claims.text: Education professors have translated their resentment of prestigious university colleagues into a resentment of the subject-matter knowledge those colleagues represent.
claims.source_passage: Professors of education, surrounded in the university by prestigious colleagues whose strong suit is thought to be knowledge, have translated resentment against this elite cadre into resentment against the knowledge from which it draws its prestige.

claims.text: Early 'normal schools' were originally oriented toward subject matter mastery rather than educational process.
claims.source_passage: These early “normal schools,” or teacher-training colleges... were oriented mainly to subject matter, on the assumption that competency in teaching depended primarily on mastery of the material to be taught and secondarily on the best methods for teaching it.

claims.text: The transition of teacher colleges into university departments led to a loss of focus on teacher training and a rise in institutional rivalry.
claims.source_passage: The nation’s gain was a serious loss to the prestige of the teacher-training unit that had been the raison d’ětre of the original institution... thus did the parent... slink away in wounded isolation.

claims.text: Education schools adopted abstract process-oriented terminology as a means of gaining 'compensatory academic standing' within the university.
claims.source_passage: attempting to give itself compensatory academic standing by high-sounding process terms borrowed from Columbia University’s Teachers College and other education schools that had experienced a similar history.

claims.text: The combination of institutional power with professional resentment in the educational establishment is socially dangerous.
claims.source_passage: It is never a healthy circumstance when people who are held in low esteem exercise dominant influence in an important sphere. The conjunction of power with resentment is deadly.

claims.text: The transformation of the original teacher-training unit into a university unit at UCLA led to a loss of prestige and internal uncertainty about its institutional identity.
claims.source_passage: The nation’s gain was a serious loss to the prestige of the teacher-training unit that had been the raison d’ětre of the original institution. With its change in title, the new university came under the charge of the central administration at Berkeley, some members of which felt that the mere training of teachers was not an activity appropriate for a true university.

claims.text: Education schools attempted to gain compensatory academic standing by adopting process-oriented terminology borrowed from institutions like Teachers College.
claims.source_passage: attempting to give itself compensatory academic standing by high-sounding process terms borrowed from Columbia University’s Teachers College and other education schools that had experienced a similar history.

claims.text: The search for professional identity in education by finding unique subject matters was largely unsuccessful because most disciplines were already claimed by other academic departments.
claims.source_passage: The search for professional identity and status by finding subject matters unique to education did not prove very successful. As James Koerner has observed, “Whatever disciplines the school of education might incorporate in educational studies were already the province of other academics. This left them only pedagogy as a unique possession.”

claims.text: The emphasis on 'process' in education schools led to a reduction in both intellectual content and interaction with the wider university.
claims.source_passage: Thus the search for identity and status led to an emphasis on process, which entailed a reduction of both intellectual content and intellectual interaction with the rest of the university.

claims.text: The decline in intellectual substance within education schools defeated the goals of institutional prestige and identity that the schools were trying to achieve.
claims.source_passage: the decline in intellectual substance, which was instituted to create a special, separate discipline, thereby defeated the very goals being aimed at—institutional prestige and an imposing institutional identity.

claims.text: Horace Mann believed that teacher-training institutions should be isolated and solely focused on their social mission to avoid the distractions of subject-matter prestige.
claims.source_passage: Horace Mann recognized as early as 1839 that teacher-training institutions were inevitably exposed to such tensions and temptations, because professors tended to be more interested in their subjects and their prestige than in their social missions.

claims.text: John Dewey's early goal for the department of pedagogy at the University of Chicago was to put knowledge in the service of the mission-oriented enterprise of education.
claims.source_passage: The idea was not to subordinate pedagogy to philosophy, but vice versa, to put knowledge in the service of the mission-oriented enterprise of education.

claims.text: Early education reformers like Mann and Dewey prioritized the pragmatic and practical over theory and scholarship.
claims.source_passage: Two features are especially notable about these early ideals of Mann and Dewey. First, they had not the slightest doubt about priorities. The pragmatic was primary; theory and scholarship in education departments were valuable only as they contributed to educational improvement and to the democratic ideals that education was to serve.

claims.text: Dewey argued that every child in a democracy requires specific training in science, art, history, and the fundamental tools of communication.
claims.source_passage: Every child in a democracy, Dewey argued, must have “training in science, in art, in history; command of the fundamental methods of inquiry and the fundamental tools of intercourse and communication … habits of industry, perseverance, and, above all, habits of serviceableness.”

claims.text: In the early ideals of teacher training, there was an assumed accommodation between pedagogy and subject-matter knowledge rather than an antagonistic rivalry.
claims.source_passage: Second, the dominance of the practical over the theoretical having been established, there was to be no internecine warfare between theory and practice, knowledge and method. The principle of accommodation between pedagogy and subject-matter disciplines was self-evident and assumed.

claims.text: The massive conversion of normal schools to teachers colleges between 1910 and 1930 created a demand for a distinct intellectual system and distinctive principles of thought for the education professoriat.
claims.source_passage: The growth of these college-level schools and departments of education required a large increase in the numbers of professors of education, which, in turn, intensified the demand for a distinct intellectual system for that professoriat—new journals, new professional associations, and, above all, distinctive principles of thought.

claims.text: Teachers College in New York City was the primary source of the distinctive intellectual system that defined American teacher training between 1910 and 1930.
claims.source_passage: To fill that demand, it was reasonable to look toward the mother of American teacher-training institutions: Teachers College in New York City, founded in 1889 and incorporated into Columbia University in 1898—the institution that trained more professors of education than any other in the fateful period between 1910 and 1930.

claims.text: Historical conditions in the 1920s led the educational community to assert itself socially and intellectually following the decline of earlier service ideals.
claims.source_passage: By the 1920s, with the decline of earlier ideals of service, historical conditions had determined that the moment had arrived for the educational community to assert itself socially and intellectually.

claims.text: Teachers College at Columbia University was the dominant institution for training education professors during the formative period between 1910 and 1930.
claims.source_passage: To fill that demand, it was reasonable to look toward the mother of American teacher-training institutions: Teachers College in New York City... the institution that trained more professors of education than any other in the fateful period between 1910 and 1930.

claims.text: William Heard Kilpatrick was the charismatic codifier of the American educational community's current ideas and its most influential figure.
claims.source_passage: I mention his name frequently in this book because although he was not the only important figure to develop the current ideas of the American educational community, he was the charismatic codifier of them.

claims.text: Kilpatrick's lineage and the sheer number of students he trained explain the relative uniformity of current American educational doctrine.
claims.source_passage: His professorial disciples subsequently perpetuated his ideas by training still more and more professors of education throughout the nation. This lineage helps explain the relative uniformity of current American educational doctrine.

claims.text: Modern pedagogical reform identifies its methods with liberal, democratic American ideals as a source of status and mission.
claims.source_passage: one can find most of the major themes of present-day pedagogical “reform” in Kilpatrick’s book: the identification of correct pedagogy with liberal, democratic American ideals

claims.text: Educational reformers since Kilpatrick have disparaged specific subject matter in favor of teaching 'children rather than subjects.'
claims.source_passage: the disparagement of mere subject matter and of other nations’ educational methods; the admonition to teach children rather than subjects

claims.text: The claim that knowledge changes too fast to require a specific curriculum is a central theme of Kilpatrick-style pedagogical reform.
claims.source_passage: the claim that knowledge is changing so fast that no specific subject matter should be required in the curriculum

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s 'process-over-all' concept enabled the education professoriat to claim professional independence from traditional academic disciplines.
claims.source_passage: It was this process-over-all concept that helped give the newly emerging professoriat its claim to an independent and separate existence.

claims.text: The idea that pedagogical method can be free from traditional knowledge and still encompass the whole of education is logically dubious.
claims.source_passage: Above all, what won general acceptance in the educational community was the idea that a new method free from the trammels of traditional knowledge could encompass the whole sphere of education by merging subject matter into the process of pedagogy.

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s 'Foundations of Method' is intellectually disappointing and fails to provide sustained argument or evidence for its pedagogical claims.
claims.source_passage: Despite its influence and resounding title, Foundations of Method is remarkably disappointing as an intellectual performance.

claims.text: The use of scientific-sounding psychological terms in educational theory often lacks a logical connection to specific pedagogical methods.
claims.source_passage: Whatever the enduring soundness of these highly generalized psychological principles, their connection to a specific pedagogical method of the sort Kilpatrick advocates is not, and cannot be, effectively argued.

claims.text: General laws of thought are too broad to justify one specific pedagogical method over another, as they apply to any method that works.
claims.source_passage: Kilpatrick does not seem to notice that the very generality of such principles can be used to justify any effective pedagogical method, since every pedagogy which works must by that very fact apply laws of thought

claims.text: Kilpatrick's educational approach explicitly rejected required learning in reading, arithmetic, and spelling, as well as examinations and grades.
claims.source_passage: The children were not to be required to learn reading, to master prescribed arithmetic or spelling: there were to he no examinations. They were not to he marked or graded in terms of a prescribed curriculum.

claims.text: William Heard Kilpatrick’s Foundations of Method is a disappointing intellectual performance despite its significant influence.
claims.source_passage: Despite its influence and resounding title, Foundations of Method is remarkably disappointing as an intellectual performance.

claims.text: The connection between generalized psychological principles and Kilpatrick's specific pedagogical method is logically non-existent and cannot be effectively argued.
claims.source_passage: Whatever the enduring soundness of these highly generalized psychological principles, their connection to a specific pedagogical method of the sort Kilpatrick advocates is not, and cannot be, effectively argued.

claims.text: The generality of universal psychological principles can be used to justify any effective pedagogical method, making them useless as a defense for one specific method.
claims.source_passage: Kilpatrick does not seem to notice that the very generality of such principles can be used to justify any effective pedagogical method, since every pedagogy which works must by that very fact apply laws of thought which are by definition universal.

claims.text: Modern proponents of psychological constructivism suffer from the same logical difficulty of using general principles to justify specific methods as Kilpatrick did.
claims.source_passage: This is a logical difficulty that continues to plague, or ought to plague, the new scientistic proponents of psychological “constructivism.”

claims.text: Pedagogical methods should ultimately be judged by their concrete results rather than theoretical alignment with psychological laws.
claims.source_passage: In the end, it is concrete results by which pedagogical methods are to be judged.

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s success as a figurehead was based on a self-aware tone of institutional leadership and scientific certitude rather than intellectual depth.
claims.source_passage: What guaranteed Kilpatrick’s success as a prophet was his self-aware tone as the leader of a new institutional dispensation.

claims.text: Kilpatrick fostered an institutional identity for educators based on a pugnacious tone of scientific certitude and professional exclusivity.
claims.source_passage: This pugnacious tone of scientific certitude, combined with a message of professional identity (which the members of the newly forming profession very much wanted to hear), carried all before it.

claims.text: Kilpatrick advocated for 'militant separatism,' which separated education as a discipline from other academic fields.
claims.source_passage: In his institutional influence, then, Kilpatrick stood for militant separatism—the separation of education from other disciplines...

claims.text: Kilpatrick intentionally separated the study of pedagogy from the study of subject matter.
claims.source_passage: ...the separation of subject matter from pedagogy...

claims.text: Kilpatrick separated 'forward-thinking social thinkers' from those he characterized as 'reactionary traditionalists.'
claims.source_passage: ...and the separation of forward-thinking social thinkers from reactionary (implicitly antidemocratic) traditionalists.

claims.text: The self-righteous militancy of the education profession established by Kilpatrick in the early 20th century is still present today.
claims.source_passage: The aggressive tone of the prophecy was well suited to its historical function of developing an identity for the profession, and this tone of self-righteous militancy is still evident today.

claims.text: The uncompromising separatism of the education field was a strategic necessity to maintain professional distinctiveness and prestige.
claims.source_passage: Historically, it may have been almost inevitable for such an uncompromising separatism to emerge victorious as the dominant attitude of the new profession. Any more conciliatory approach might jeopardize its professional distinctiveness and prestige.

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s militancy was a radical departure from the earlier American educational ideals of Horace Mann and Thomas Jefferson.
claims.source_passage: But in historical perspective this militancy was a new departure for educational thinking in the United States. The noble ideals under which Teachers College was founded were continuations of the communitarian spirit of Horace Mann and, indirectly, of Jefferson.

claims.text: Kilpatrick explicitly rejected the goal of education as a means of cultural transmission and social perpetuation.
claims.source_passage: Kilpatrick explicitly rejected the ideal of education as transmission and social perpetuation.

claims.text: Kilpatrick viewed modern American civilization as too 'dynamic' for children to be taught the traditions or customs of the past.
claims.source_passage: By contrast, modern America represented a “dynamic” civilization in which children were not to be slaves of the past, but made into independent thinkers who, unburdened by earlier customs and prejudices, would stand ready to face the future.

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s educational philosophy was significantly more individualized and 'rootless' than that of John Dewey.
claims.source_passage: This was a far more individualized and rootless view of education than Dewey, whom Kilpatrick continually invoked, had proposed.

claims.text: John Dewey believed the foundational aim of education is to ensure social continuity through the transmission of the group's aims and habits.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, the opening pages of Democracy and Education identifies the foundational aim of education as the renewing of the social group.… Education in its broadest sense is the means of this social continuity of life.

claims.text: Failure to ensure the thorough transmission of knowledge between generations leads a civilization into barbarism and savagery.
claims.source_passage: Unless pains are taken to see that genuine and thorough transmission takes place, the most civilized group will relapse into barbarism and then into savagery.

claims.text: Much of what critics attack as aimless and contentless 'Deweyism' was in fact aimless and contentless 'Kilpatrickism.'
claims.source_passage: [M]uch of what critics then (and now) attacked as aimless, contentless “Deweyism” was in fact aimless, contentless “Kilpatrickism.”

claims.text: Despite his public stance, Dewey was critical of Kilpatrick’s 'project method' for its lack of focus on mastering organized subjects.
claims.source_passage: “Though Dewey rarely named names in his criticisms of progressive reform,” writes Robert Westbrook... “one of his principal targets was William H. Kilpatrick... [Dewey] insisted that projects must have as one of their goals the child’s mastery of organized subjects.”

claims.text: William H. Kilpatrick’s 'project method' was the single most influential practical curricular reform to emerge from child-centered progressivism.
claims.source_passage: one of his principal targets was William H. Kilpatrick, his colleague at Columbia University, whose “project method” was perhaps the single most influential practical curricular reform to emerge from child-centered progressivism.

claims.text: John Dewey insisted that educational projects must include the child’s mastery of organized subjects as a primary goal.
claims.source_passage: But [Dewey] insisted that projects must have as one of their goals the child’s mastery of organized subjects.

claims.text: The most profound thinkers in the American educational tradition have been integrationists who acknowledge the necessity of cultural transmission and continuity.
claims.source_passage: Dewey, and indeed all the profoundest thinkers in the American educational tradition, have been integrationists, not separatists. First, they have been integrationists with respect to the past, acknowledging the centrality of transmission and continuity in education.

claims.text: Training the mind through vague, future-oriented 'critical thinking' methods cannot replace subject-matter knowledge or communal learning.
claims.source_passage: never advocating the idea that training the mind by a vague, future-oriented method directed to “critical thinking” can replace either subject-matter knowledge or the common learnings that constitute the life of the community.

claims.text: William Bagley was the true continuer of the Horace Mann tradition of American education.
claims.source_passage: In this light, the true continuer of what was best and deepest in the Horace Mann tradition was not Kilpatrick but his less influential colleague at Teachers College, William Bagley.

claims.text: A primary function of formal education in a democracy is to ensure a high level of common culture and shared meanings.
claims.source_passage: A most important function of formal education, especially in a democracy, is to insure as high a level of common culture as possible—meanings, understandings, standards, and aspirations common to a large proportion of the democratic group

claims.text: Collective thinking and decision-making in a democracy require a high measure of common elements in school programs across the country.
claims.source_passage: to the end that the collective thinking and the collective decisions of the group may be done and made on the highest possible plane. This obviously calls for a goodly measure of common elements in the school programs throughout the country

claims.text: William Bagley’s educational vision failed because it lacked the 'professional distinctiveness' required to form an autonomous pedagogical discipline.
claims.source_passage: The failure of Bagley’s accommodating, communitarian vision to win over his colleagues was mainly owing to its integrationism and lack of professional distinctiveness.

claims.text: Kilpatrick’s proposals succeeded because they obeyed the institutional imperative to create a jargon-heavy, guild-specialist vocabulary for educators.
claims.source_passage: Instead of an autonomous, process-oriented expertise and a jargon vocabulary that made guild specialists of educators (in the way Kilpatrick’s proposals did), Bagley offered his colleagues a more complex vision

claims.text: The fundamental unsoundness of the Kilpatrick approach is the cause of the public and academic contempt directed toward education experts.
claims.source_passage: For, in the end, it has been the fundamental unsoundness of the Kilpatrick approach that has generated the suspicion—often mixed with contempt—with which education professors and experts are often regarded

claims.text: Individual educators often privately value knowledge even while their professional context forces them to publicly disparage it.
claims.source_passage: For, outside of their professional context, few educators privately retain the hostility to knowledge and community that their profession has taught them to enunciate.

claims.text: Healthy professional schools like law, medicine, and engineering thrive on intellectual cross-fertilization with other academic departments.
claims.source_passage: In many universities, these professional schools thrive on the intellectual ferment of cross-fertilization and joint appointments between departments.

claims.text: Education schools interpret the idea of joint appointments with other departments as a sociological threat to their autonomy and self-esteem rather than an intellectual opportunity.
claims.source_passage: The move is interpreted in entirely sociological terms as a threat to autonomy and self-esteem, and as a matter of academic politics, with no intellectual justification or interest

claims.text: Successful professional schools like law, medicine, and engineering maintain a collegial and cooperative relationship with the subject-matter disciplines to which they are related.
claims.source_passage: In many universities, these professional schools thrive on the intellectual ferment of cross-fertilization and joint appointments between departments.

claims.text: Education schools exist in a state of 'glum and resentful isolation' from other academic departments.
claims.source_passage: Contrast that condition of cooperation with the glum and resentful isolation of education schools.

claims.text: The belief that subject-matter disciplines are governed by status norms rather than by advancing knowledge is an unhealthy view rejected by successful professional schools.
claims.source_passage: The idea that subject-matter disciplines are governed by “academic and status norms” rather than by advancing knowledge is a view that healthy professional schools like law and medicine simply do not accept.

claims.text: The idea that educational psychology is separate from and independent of the general field of psychology is 'dangerous nonsense' that hinders educational progress.
claims.source_passage: The contrary idea in education schools that, for example, educational psychology is separate from and independent of the general field of psychology is dangerous nonsense which holds back the progress of the educational mission itself.

claims.text: There is no possibility of adequate improvement in schooling as long as influential experts hold both their current 'antiknowledge' ideas and their positions as trainers of teachers.
claims.source_passage: There is no possibility of adequate improvement in the quality of schooling so long as these influential experts continue to hold both their current ideas and their influential positions as trainers of teachers and administrators.

claims.text: The best hope for improving the educational system is to ground pedagogy in the most reliable sources, namely the subject-matter disciplines.
claims.source_passage: The best hope for creating a first-rate educational system is to ground it in ideas from the most reliable sources, namely the subject-matter disciplines.

claims.text: The integrationist tradition represented by Dewey and Bagley is the soundest tradition of pedagogical thinking in the United States.
claims.source_passage: The integrationist tradition represented by Dewey, Bagley, and others was the finest and soundest tradition of pedagogical thinking in the United States.

claims.text: Integrationism is intellectually superior to separatism because its ideas emerged from free exchange and scientific criticism.
claims.source_passage: The practical superiority of integrationism over separatism in education is due mainly to its inherent intellectual superiority. Its ideas, which emerged from the rough-and-tumble of free exchange and scientific criticism, are not only more likely to be the truest ideas but are more likely than defective ones to work.

claims.text: The misleading ideas of the educational Thoughtworld arise from three main sources: American exceptionalism, Romanticism, and professional separatism.
claims.source_passage: From the powerful historical and social forces that I’ve called American exceptionalism, Romanticism, and professional separatism, and from their subcategories, have arisen almost all the misleading ideas of the Thoughtworld.

claims.text: The claim that specific subject matter is unnecessary because knowledge changes too fast is a 'fatuous' idea invented by Kilpatrick in the 1920s.
claims.source_passage: For instance, the fatuous claim that knowledge is changing so fast that specific subject matter need not be a critical component of education seems to have been invented by Kilpatrick in the 1920s.

claims.text: The 'knowledge-is-changing-fast' slogan was not a logical necessity but a convenience that aligned with Romantic and separatist impulses.
claims.source_passage: While his slogan was not a necessary inference from either Romanticism or professional separatism, it coincided with the impulses of both. It fit educational Romanticism by emphasizing individual growth above conventional knowledge.

claims.text: The specific form of modern educational ideas was not inevitable but was shaped by historical accidents and slogans.
claims.source_passage: It was not inevitable, however, that the inadequate ideas of the educational community should have taken the particular form they did. Certain slogans undoubtedly came into existence by historical accident.

claims.text: The 'fast-changing knowledge' slogan gained currency because it aligned with the existing impulses of Romanticism, professional separatism, and American exceptionalism.
claims.source_passage: While his slogan was not a necessary inference from either Romanticism or professional separatism, it coincided with the impulses of both. It fit educational Romanticism by emphasizing individual growth above conventional knowledge, and it fit separatism by derogating the subject-matter knowledge to which other, noneducational experts had a more credible claim.

claims.text: American exceptionalism was used to contrast 'slavish' European factual education with 'independent-minded' American critical thinking.
claims.source_passage: It even fit American exceptionalism by contrasting merely factual, slavish European education with independent-minded, critical-thinking American education, appropriate for a free people.

claims.text: The three leading intellectual impulses of the Thoughtworld—Romanticism, exceptionalism, and separatism—have been deleterious to education.
claims.source_passage: If we judge ideas by their consequences, it is reasonable to conclude that the three leading intellectual impulses of the Thoughtworld have been as wrong as they have been deleterious.

claims.text: Romantic theories of education are fundamentally incorrect despite the value of Romanticism in literature.
claims.source_passage: Romanticism may have created some of the greatest poetry in our language, but its theories of education have been wrong theories.

claims.text: American exceptionalism in education leads to a complacency that avoids learning from the successful educational experiences of other nations.
claims.source_passage: exceptionalism can become mere complacency that evades the challenge of learning from the experiences of other peoples.

claims.text: Extreme professionalism in the education field has resulted in group grievance and a self-protective mentality that avoids accountability.
claims.source_passage: extreme professionalism becomes narrow and separatist. It results in a sense of group grievance and in a self-protective mentality that evades responsibility.

claims.text: The educational tradition established by Horace Mann was replaced by the anti-knowledge tradition of William Heard Kilpatrick.
claims.source_passage: The tradition of Horace Mann got submerged in the tradition of William Heard Kilpatrick. Idealistic practicality gave way to a militant separatism that expanded its half-truths to cover the whole educational landscape.

claims.text: American education has been dominated by 'antiknowledge extremism' for six decades.
claims.source_passage: Now, after six decades of antiknowledge extremism, it is unclear whether the public needs to oppose this defective tradition with a countervailing extremism that repudiates process in favor of knowledge, or whether it might be possible to reintroduce a tradition of accommodation whose hallmarks are skepticism, openness, and practicality.

claims.text: The early structured schooling required in Europe and Asia shows no documented harmful psychological effects on students.
claims.source_passage: The lack of harmful psychological effects is documented in both Europe and Asia. For Europe, the most authoritative work, since it comprises data from twenty thousand students, is Duthoit, “L’Enfant et l’école,” 3–13.

claims.text: For Plato, the recently popular motto 'Follow your bliss' would have constituted a 'moral blasphemy' because it prioritizes instinctive preferences over the governance of reason.
claims.source_passage: The recently popular motto “Follow your bliss,” where “bliss” means whatever your instinctive preferences happen to be, would have seemed to Plato a moral blasphemy.

claims.text: St. Augustine’s pear tree narrative specifically identifies the perversity of human nature through the act of picking more fruit than could be eaten, proving the theft was nihilistic rather than for sustenance.
claims.source_passage: More significant to Augustine was the fact that they picked many more pears than they could possibly eat, perversely carrying them away to throw to pigs. The only explanation for such a nihilistic and destructive act, Augustine thought, was that even in the young child... there are an evil and a perversity explainable only by the doctrine of original sin.

claims.text: The 'Virgin Land' archetype within American exceptionalism implies that Americans are free from the 'tainted' history of the Old World and, by extension, free from original sin.
claims.source_passage: The “stuff untainted” of our Western soil meant that we were free from the burden of the past—even free, by implication, from original sin. Freedom from a tainted past, celebration of the vast pure landscape, and a derogation of traditions and books—all were very American, very exceptionalist versions of Romanticism.

claims.text: Karl Jaspers’ principle of worldview study suggests that accurate insight can be gained from either a position of attraction or repudiation toward a thoughtworld.
claims.source_passage: In his book Psychology of Worldviews, Karl Jaspers observed that one cannot well understand a thoughtworld without being motivated by attraction or repudiation. But he shrewdly added that either approach can lead to accurate understanding and insight.

claims.text: A pedagogical approach that synthesizes 'showing' (indirect, lifelike) and 'telling' (direct, focused) is superior to using either method exclusively.
claims.source_passage: But a combination of showing and telling yields the fastest and securest results in both writing and teaching. For most subjects, a pedagogy that mixes indirect, lifelike methods with direct, focused ones will yield the highest student interest and attainment.

claims.text: Knowledge is not rendered useless simply because a child does not yet possess a complete understanding of the object or concept being studied.
claims.source_passage: We should never imagine, because a child does not understand everything about an object, that what he knows is useless to him.

claims.text: The author accepts the cultivation of independent-mindedness as a valid and positive American educational goal, provided it is not decoupled from the mastery of factual knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Of course, there are some tenets of American exceptionalism to which most of us continue to subscribe. We are justifiably proud of the degree to which our children are encouraged to think for themselves, to be independent-minded and critical of received ideas.

claims.text: The evolution of the Los Angeles State Normal School into UCLA represents a sociological pattern where the new university structure 'spit upon its parent' by derogating the teacher-training mission that founded it.
claims.source_passage: Thus did the child (the new university, UCLA) spit upon its parent (the Normal School). And thus did the parent... slink away in wounded isolation, consoling itself with the nobility of its vocation.

claims.text: Noah Webster acted as a vigorous American exceptionalist by arguing that American spelling should be intentionally modified to reflect the nation's unique identity.
claims.source_passage: One of the first great American educators, Noah Webster, was a vigorous exceptionalist who insisted that even our spelling should reflect our uniqueness.

claims.text: The College Board's 'nonlinear transformation' of SAT scores was a self-protective measure designed to preserve the board's own self-esteem rather than address educational reality.
claims.source_passage: It is as though the College Board had turned the illusionist therapy the educational community practices on students upon itself in order to preserve its own self-esteem.

evidence.description: Mainstream historical and scientific research is cited as being in conflict with the dogmas of the educational world.
The names on the frieze of the Horace Mann building at Teachers College (Loyola, Melanchthon, Pestalozzi, etc.) representing the evolution of educational theory.
St. Augustine's account of stealing pears in his Confessions used as an illustration of the doctrine of original sin.
The constitutional structures of the Senate, separation of powers, and protections against the majority as evidence of the Founders' skepticism of human nature.
The structural design of the US Constitution (Senate, separation of powers) as a mechanism to check human greed.
Thomas Jefferson's writings emphasizing moral education and the study of history as essential for republican citizenship.
evidence.source_reference: Jefferson, The Life and Selected Writings of Thomas Jefferson, 265

evidence.description: Friedrich Froebel's writings comparing children to plants that should unfold naturally without interference.
Johann Pestalozzi's warnings against hurrying children into conventional learning or remote interests.
Wordsworth's poetry characterizing childhood as 'heaven-born freedom' and social custom as a 'weight' heavy as frost.
evidence.source_reference: Wordsworth, “Ode: Intimations of Immortality from Recollections of Early Childhood”

evidence.description: William Wordsworth's 'Ode: Intimations of Immortality' is cited to illustrate the Romantic view of custom as a heavy, deadening weight on the soul.
evidence.source_reference: Wordsworth, 'Ode: Intimations of Immortality from Recollections of Early Childhood,' lines 125–32
evidence.source_passage: Full soon thy Soul shall have her earthly freight, / And custom lie upon thee with a weight, / Heavy as frost, and deep almost as life!

evidence.description: Bronson Alcott's poetry is cited as evidence of the extreme Romantic belief in the 'infallible divinity' of the child.
evidence.source_passage: Unsealed, mine eyes behold all outward things / Arrayed in splendors of divinity.

evidence.description: Colonel Francis Parker's address to teachers where he defines the child's spontaneous tendencies as records of inborn divinity.
evidence.source_reference: Quoted in Cremin, The Transformation of the School, 134–5
evidence.source_passage: The spontaneous tendencies of the child are the records of inborn divinity. We are here, my fellow teachers, for one purpose, and that purpose is to understand these tendencies and continue them in all these directions, following nature.

evidence.description: William Heard Kilpatrick's writings which describe 'purposeful activity' freed from subject matter as 'nature's road to mental health.'
evidence.source_reference: Kilpatrick, 'The Essentials of the Activity Movement.' Quoted in Tennenbaum, William Heard Kilpatrick
evidence.source_passage: A well-managed regime of purposeful activity, freed from artificial and external demands of subject matter requirements... is nature’s road to mental health.

evidence.description: The concluding scene of Mark Twain's Huckleberry Finn, where Huck rejects civilization ('sivilize') in favor of 'the Territory.'
evidence.source_reference: Twain, Huckleberry Finn, last words of the book
evidence.source_passage: But I reckon I got to light out for the Territory ahead of the rest, because Aunt Sally she’s going to adopt me and sivilize me, and I can’t stand it. I been there before.

evidence.description: The standoff between Aunt Sally and Huck Finn in Huckleberry Finn representing the conflict between Enlightenment civilization and Romantic naturalism.
evidence.source_passage: The conflict in American culture between the two traditions is wonderfully memorialized in Huckleberry Finn in the standoff between Aunt Sally... and Huck Finn.

evidence.description: Historical timeline of SAT score declines corresponding with the first generation of progressive-educated seniors.
The shift in primary school reading materials from McGuffey Readers to fairy tales.
evidence.source_reference: Cremin, The Transformation of the School

evidence.description: The popularity of anti-academic parenting and education books.
evidence.source_reference: David Elkind, The Hurried Child and Miseducation
evidence.source_passage: Popular books with titles like David Elkind’s The Hurried Child: Growing Up Too Fast Too Soon and Miseducation: Preschoolers at Risk tell many Americans what they are already disposed to hear.

evidence.description: Longitudinal study of 4,000 French children regarding the effects of starting school at age two.
evidence.source_reference: Jarousee, Mingat, and Richard, 'La Scolarisation maternelle à deux ans,' 55
evidence.source_passage: Recently, French social scientists completed longitudinal studies of some four thousand children on the long-term effects of écoles maternelles on the more than 30 percent of French two-year-olds who now attend these preschools.

evidence.description: Attendance rates in French preschools: 80% of 3-year-olds, 95% of 4-year-olds, and 99% of 5-year-olds.
Comparison between outcomes of disadvantaged children in French preschools vs. those in US programs like Head Start.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (referencing Chapter 1 and French longitudinal data)

evidence.description: Neurobiological research regarding brain development and early learning.
evidence.source_passage: If one also brings in studies from neurobiology, the claim that early education requires extreme gradualness and hands-off spontaneity is refuted by research that has determined what “nature” is actually telling us about the brain’s development.

evidence.description: Neurobiological data on synaptic density and brain 'chiseling' through functional validation and selective elimination.
evidence.source_reference: Pasko Rakic, cited in Life magazine (1994)
evidence.source_passage: Children’s brains can make far more synaptic connections than can adults’. Shortly after birth the brain makes connections at an incredible pace. ... Then two processes begin—functional validation, in which the connections the brain finds useful are made permanent, and selective elimination.

evidence.description: Cross-national comparison of math curricula showing French and Japanese children mastering 5-digit subtraction and multiplication tables by third grade.
evidence.source_reference: National curriculum guides for France and Japan; Geary, Children’s Mathematical Development
evidence.source_passage: By the end of third grade, they can add and subtract five-digit numbers... all without great strain on them or their teachers and without any known harmful psychological effects.

evidence.description: Linguistic evidence regarding the biological window for accent-free language acquisition before puberty.
evidence.source_passage: Linguists have marveled that children can learn a new language without an accent, while adults cannot. ... Control of these muscles is in the synapses that were formed before puberty.

evidence.description: A study of 20,000 students in Europe showing no harmful psychological effects from early structured academic instruction.
evidence.source_passage: The lack of harmful psychological effects is documented in both Europe and Asia.

evidence.description: Video evidence of Japanese math instruction showing the process of learning place value to be 'painless and even fun.'
evidence.source_passage: There is vivid evidence that the process is painless, and even fun. See the highly informative video of Japanese instruction in mathematics produced by Harold Stevenson and his colleagues.

evidence.description: A list of ten major American professional associations (ASCD, NCTM, etc.) supporting the position statement to delay academic content.
Comparison to educational experts in other countries who successfully teach place value in early grades.
Jean Piaget's explicit cautions that environment can play a decisive role and that stages are not immutably fixed.
evidence.source_reference: Piaget, Science of Education and the Psychology of the Child, 173
evidence.source_passage: that environment can play a decisive role in the development of the mind; that the thought content of the stages and the ages at which they occur are not immutably fixed

evidence.description: Evidence that early math instruction in Japan is painless and effective.
NAEYC Guidelines recommending themes and projects over abstract concepts like place value for young children.
evidence.source_reference: National Association for the Education of Young People, 'Guidelines for Appropriate Curriculum Content and Assessment' (1991)
evidence.source_passage: Conceptual organizers such as themes, units, or projects give children something meaningful and substantive to engage their minds.

evidence.description: Studies showing that a mix of instructional behaviors improves teacher effectiveness and student achievement.
evidence.source_reference: Brophy and Good, 'Teacher Behavior and Student Achievement' (1986); Walberg, 'Improving the Productivity of America’s Schools' (1986)

evidence.description: Neurobiological evidence that distributed practice is essential for storing motor skills and semantic information in long-term memory.
evidence.source_passage: distributed practice—an hour a day for seven days—is much more effective for both types of memory than seven hours in a row of massed practice.

evidence.description: Transcultural studies showing language development follows an invariant, universal pattern.
evidence.source_reference: Not explicitly named in text; refers to general transcultural studies
evidence.source_passage: The model has also been gaining a lot of empirical support from transcultural studies of language development; language growth in every culture apparently follows an invariant pattern.

evidence.description: The collective professional experience of teachers in French écoles maternelles regarding early childhood timing.
evidence.source_reference: Norvez, De la naissance à l’école
evidence.source_passage: What has been concluded by the teachers at the écoles maternelles, who have more collective experience with the two-to-five age group than any other single body of professionals in the world? They avoid dogmatism in either direction on questions of timing.

evidence.description: Experimental findings showing that animals subjected to stress as infants performed better on learning tasks as adults compared to nonstressed controls.
evidence.source_passage: Experimental animals given “stress experiences as infants performed better on learning tasks after they were adults than did nonstressed controls.”

evidence.description: International comparison of educational achievement in successful systems (Europe and Asia) showing that extra effort does not cause psychological harm.
evidence.source_passage: Children, parents, and teachers in such systems are made explicitly aware of the minimal knowledge and skills the children are required to attain at each grade, and everyone cooperates in putting forth the effort needed to reach that goal.

evidence.description: Comparison of the correlation between social class and academic talent in the United States versus France, noting the US parallelism is much higher.
evidence.source_reference: Not explicitly named in this snippet, but referenced as 'demonstrable'
evidence.source_passage: In the United States, there demonstrably exists a too-neat parallelism between “innate” academic talent and social class—a parallelism that is far greater than in France and far greater than statistical variation could justify.

evidence.description: Statistics on the percentage of non-naturalized, non-French students in Paris region schools (23.2%).
evidence.source_reference: Ministère de l’Éducation Nationale, 'Les Éleves de nationalité étrangère scolarisés dans le premier et le second degré en 1993–1994'
evidence.source_passage: Currently, in the Paris region, the total percentage of nonnaturalized, non-French students is 23.2 percent of the school population.

evidence.description: A ten-year distillation of comparative studies of education in Taiwan, Japan, and the United States published in refereed scientific journals.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson and Stigler, The Learning Gap (1992)
evidence.source_passage: distillation of comparative studies of education in different countries conducted over a ten-year period by the authors and their numerous colleagues in Taiwan, Japan, and the United States.

evidence.description: A study showing that schoolbooks from 1790-1900 contrasted American virtue with European decadence.
evidence.source_passage: Ruth Elson, in her study of the books used in our schools from 1790 to 1900, showed that they unanimously contrasted virtuous and natural Americans with corrupt and decadent Europeans.

evidence.description: James Russell Lowell's 'Commemoration Ode' (1865) praising Lincoln's unique Americanness and nature's 'new mould'.
evidence.source_passage: For him her Old-World moulds aside she threw, / And choosing sweet clay from the breast / Of the unexhausted West, / With stuff untainted shaped a hero new

evidence.description: Sales figures for A.S. Neill's 'Summerhill' indicating its mass popularity in the US.
evidence.source_passage: By 1969, according to Diane Ravitch, Summerhill was selling in the United States at a rate of more than two hundred thousand copies a year.

evidence.description: The 1987 vote by the British Parliament to introduce a national curriculum despite professional resistance.
evidence.source_passage: the free fall into process was halted by the British Parliament in 1987, when, against the resistance of educational professionals, it voted to introduce a national curriculum into the schools.

evidence.description: The Anglo-American Dartmouth Conferences that influenced language arts toward process-oriented instruction.
evidence.source_passage: the Anglo-American Dartmouth Conferences, which ratified the idea of turning language arts instruction in a process-oriented direction.

evidence.description: British adoption of a national curriculum in 1987 following observation of higher-performing systems on the Continent.
The historical move in the US from local currencies and sundial time to national currency and time zones.
Post-WWII Japanese rejection of American recommendations for local educational governance.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson and Stigler, The Learning Gap, Chapter 7

evidence.description: The Danish system ensuring all schools are equally good through commonality, recounted by a scholar colleague.
The correlation in other nations between high teacher prestige/pay and the existence of a common curriculum.
Nations with content commonality show high teacher prestige and salaries.
Tocqueville's observations on the longstanding strength of independent and skeptical elements in American culture.
Barry Shain's historical analysis showing that American individualism shifted significantly after 1850 due to European Romanticism.
evidence.source_reference: Shain, Barry. The Myth of American Individualism (1994)
evidence.source_passage: Barry Shain... has shown in The Myth of American Individualism that our present ideas of individualism are quite different from those current in the United States before 1850.

evidence.description: Edward Young's 1759 essay arguing that genius is innate and 'not made,' which Hirsch identifies as the root of modern academic leniency.
evidence.source_reference: Young, Edward. Conjectures on Original Composition (1759)
evidence.source_passage: each original composition 'rises spontaneously from the vital root of genius; it grows, it is not made.'

evidence.description: A correlation of .17 between academic self-esteem and academic accomplishment.
The College Board's 'non-linear transformation' of the verbal SAT mean from 425 to 500.
A comparison of educational attainment between blacks in Jamaica and the United States, showing higher achievement in Jamaica despite lower material resources.
evidence.source_passage: Given their similar cultural and genetic origins, and their similar historical degradation in slavery, why is the educational attainment of West Indian blacks significantly higher than that of U.S. blacks, and why does it produce a statistically remarkable number of distinguished African-Americans of Caribbean origin

evidence.description: Personal testimony regarding West Indian one-room schoolhouses with large class sizes (averaging 86 students) succeeding through the assumption of teachability.
evidence.source_passage: The fundamental assumption in the Caribbean on the part of those Black teachers who taught me throughout elementary school—in classes that averaged eighty-six students in one-room schoolhouses... was that we were teachable.

evidence.description: Research on the social importance of self-esteem and its lack of connection to actual achievement.
evidence.source_reference: Dawes, House of Cards; Mecca, Smelser, and Vasconcellos, The Social Importance of Self Esteem
evidence.source_passage: The only sure method of making children self-confident about their work in schools (which will help their general self-esteem) is, as Patterson and Dawes suggest, to nurture the hard work that will lead to actual academic achievement.

evidence.description: Howard Gardner's book 'Frames of Mind' (1983) which introduced the seven independent intelligences.
The chemical formula for water (H2O) used as an example of scientific consensus reliability.
George A. Miller's 1983 review in the New York Times Book Review critiquing Gardner's catalogue of intelligences.
George Miller's critique of the psychometric validity of Howard Gardner's Multiple Intelligences.
Report on the scathing criticism of Virginia's draft guidelines for grade-by-grade knowledge standards.
Richard Hofstadter's definition and analysis of anti-intellectualism in American life.
Walt Whitman's poem 'When I Heard the Learn’d Astronomer' as evidence of American fatigue with book learning.
Tocqueville's observation of the American faith in the diffusion of knowledge for the perfectibility of man.
Emerson's journal entry from September 14, 1839, criticizing Horace Mann’s address on education.
The report 'Cardinal Principles of Secondary Education' (1918), which prioritized nonacademic goals.
William Heard Kilpatrick's 'Foundations of Method' (1925) calling for the end of separate school subjects.
The observation that children learn approximately eight new words per day through an active, hypothesis-making process.
The 1987 film 'Stand and Deliver' depicts mathematics as the primary path for socio-economic mobility for disadvantaged students.
A dialogue where a five-year-old child attempts to interpret the phrase 'Your Highness' based on literal height, demonstrating active hypothesis-making in language acquisition.
Isabel Beck's research on the effects of long-term vocabulary instruction on lexical access and reading comprehension.
Anecdote of a five-year-old child interpreting 'Your Highness' as a comment on their physical height ('I'm a little taller').
Pestalozzi's firsthand account of children successfully mastering geography charts and scientific propositions through rote repetition before they could fully understand them.
Linguistic examples of the words for 'chair' and 'table' in English, German (Stuhl, Tisch), and French (chaise, table).
Research showing that skills are not transferred from one domain to another (e.g., Latin does not teach general thinking).
The sociological status and history of education schools in research universities.
evidence.source_reference: Clifford and Guthrie, Ed School: A Brief for Professional Education

evidence.description: Derek Bok's failed attempts to raise the campus status of Harvard's school of education.
The persistence of the academic and economic gap despite decades of 'practical' and 'critical thinking' curricula.
evidence.source_reference: Not specified in this section but referenced as 'what in fact occurred'

evidence.description: Evidence of chronic status deprivation among education professors provided by even their defenders.
evidence.source_reference: Clifford and Guthrie, Ed School: A Brief for Professional Education
evidence.source_passage: Even their defenders, like Geraldine J. Clifford and James W. Guthrie... speak of “their multifaceted and chronic status deprivation.”

evidence.description: Historical critique of education schools' 'absurd' focus on technique and removal of knowledge.
evidence.source_passage: In 1916, Abraham Flexner wrote that education schools had “lost their heads” when they called for the removal of important knowledge as well as trivia from the school curriculum.

evidence.description: Mention of universities (Johns Hopkins, Yale, Duke) closing education schools during budget cuts.
evidence.source_passage: education schools continue to be among the first place universities look for belt-tightening, and in some places, notably Johns Hopkins, Yale, and Duke, such schools have disappeared.

evidence.description: The transformation of Los Angeles State Normal School into UCLA as a case of status transition.
evidence.source_passage: In due course, it became a four-year university, and in 1927 its name was changed from the Los Angeles State Normal School to the University of California at Los Angeles.
